description: Replace the missing root bun run e2e command with a real orchestrator for the smoke and hp-j30 Playwright suites. Install/document Playwright host dependencies or make every suite skip consistently when libgbm/libgtk are absent; avoid one suite failing while another skips. Emit structured pass/fail/skip evidence. Acceptance: bun run e2e exists at repo root, runs both desktop smoke and hp-j30 e2e, and reports deterministic skip reasons or green tests on CI.
close_reason: shipped

title: Phase 1: Vendor t3code three-store settings system + atomic-write + PubSub + relaunch
description:
WHAT
====
Lift t3code's three-store settings split + hot-reload + atomic file write + PubSub change stream + relaunchDesktopApp(reason). Three separate JSON files under ~/.hoopoe/userdata/:
- daemon-settings.json — daemon-side, hot-reloaded via fs.watch + 100ms debounce + atomic write + PubSub (Phase 2 daemon-side)
- desktop-settings.json — boot-only (read on launch; restart required for changes)
- client-settings.json — IPC-only (renderer state)

WHY (§3 lifted patterns, Appendix B)
======================================
T3code already solved: atomic write (tempfile + fsync + rename), file-watch debounce (100ms is the chosen threshold), forward-compat strip-defaults, restart-on-toggle UX, PubSub change stream. Reimplementing means re-discovering those edge cases.

This bead is the DESKTOP-SIDE work. The daemon-side hot-reload runs in Phase 2 and shares the patterns.

ADAPTATIONS
===========
- ~/.t3/userdata/ → ~/.hoopoe/userdata/.
- Schema swap to HoopoeDesktopSettings + HoopoeClientSettings + HoopoeDaemonSettings (the daemon shape comes in Phase 2, but the renderer must be ready to read it).
- stripDefaultServerSettings → stripDefaultDaemonSettings (forward-compat: don't write keys that match defaults, so future-default changes propagate without explicit migration).
- relaunchDesktopApp(reason) — the API is unchanged; the UI prompt should explain WHY restart is needed for desktop-settings changes.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §3 — settings system three-store split.
- Appendix B — files lifted: apps/server/src/serverSettings.ts (daemon, Phase 2) + apps/server/src/atomicWrite.ts (shared pattern).
- §10.3 — schemaVersion fields on every persisted file; daemon startup runs migrations with backup/rollback.

KEY-LIFE TIME RULES
====================
- desktop-settings.json — channels (latest/nightly), telemetry consent (opt-in), local-clone size cap defaults, app theme — read at boot only.
- client-settings.json — UI state: stage you were on, panel widths, last project, Activity panel persistent flag.
- daemon-settings.json (read by desktop too, but writes flow through daemon RPC) — bind addresses, log levels, build queue policy, model-context-policy defaults.

CONSIDERATIONS
==============
- Atomic write: tempfile + fsync + rename (§3 lifted patterns).
- File-watch debounce: 100ms is t3code's chosen value; do not modify without measuring.
- Schema-validated reads — if a settings file has a bad schemaVersion, fall back to defaults and warn in Diagnostics rather than crash.
- relaunchDesktopApp shows a confirm dialog ('Settings changed — restart now?'). Single-window only for v1; multi-window relaunch is post-MVP.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- apps/desktop/src/main/SettingsBridge.ts implements the three-store split + hot-reload + relaunch.
- Schema validation against types from packages/schemas/.
- Atomic-write tested with a fault-injection test (kill writer mid-write; original file unchanged).
- File-watch debounce tested (100 rapid writes in <1s → 1 reload event).
- Restart-on-toggle UX demonstrated for at least one desktop-settings field (e.g., update channel).
close_reason: Vendored t3code's atomicWrite + stripDefaults patterns (apps/desktop/src/vendored/t3code/settings/{atomicWrite,stripDefaults,index}.ts; MIT attribution preserved; Effect harness stripped). Rewrote SettingsBridge as a true three-tier resolver (defaults / user / project) with project overrides user overrides defaults; setUserSettings + setProjectSettings persist stripped+schema-versioned content via fsync atomic write; resolved() snapshot deep-merged; subscribe() streams diffed key paths with BOUNDED queue (Appendix B #1); RELAUNCH_KEYS auto-fires the relaunchDesktopApp(reason) hook on daemon.daemonBinaryPath / desktop.serverExposureMode changes; schemaVersion mismatch + malformed JSON fall back to defaults + warn; fs.watch on parent dir + basename filter survives atomic-rename writes; 100ms debounce coalesces bursts (proven by deterministic scheduleReloadForTesting test). 18 tests pass; full desktop suite 82 pass; lint clean for my code; UBS clean

title: Phase 10: Skill loader (jsm preferred with SHA-256 pin; jfp fallback; .hoopoe/skills.lock.json)
description:
WHAT
====
Skill loader for Layer 4. Dual-path resolution: jsm (Jeffrey's Skills.md CLI) preferred when subscription configured; jfp (Jeffrey's Prompts, ACFS-installed, free) fallback. Per-project SHA-256 pinning in .hoopoe/skills.lock.json (jsm) or advisory version-string (jfp).

WHY (§8.1, §10.3, §12 Phase 10)
=================================
Skills are content; Hoopoe never reimplements skill-fetch logic. When jeffreys-skills.md evolves, jsm or jfp brings the changes in without a Hoopoe code release. SHA-256 deterministic versioning lets users pin specific skill commits per project; cross-device sync keeps multi-workstation users aligned. Reproducibility + export/restore guarantees (§10.3, §10.4) depend on the lock file.

RESOLUTION ORDER (§12 Phase 10 'Skill loader')
================================================
1. Check .hoopoe/skills.lock.json for pinned SHA-256s; verify against jsm cache.
2. If jsm unavailable or skill not in premium catalog, try jfp.
3. If both fail, refuse to launch swarm; surface missing skills in Diagnostics with one-click install/update buttons for whichever installer is configured.

LOCK FILE SHAPE (§10.3, §10.4)
================================
.hoopoe/skills.lock.json:
{
  schemaVersion: 1,
  skills: [
    {id: 'vibing-with-ntm', installer: 'jsm', digest: 'sha256:...', pinnedAt, pinnedBy},
    {id: 'ntm', installer: 'jfp', version: 'v1.2.3', advisory: true, pinnedAt, pinnedBy}
  ]
}

VERIFICATION
==============
- jsm-pinned skills: SHA-256 verified before each tending run starts. Digest mismatch puts job into BLOCKED state with Diagnostics entry rather than silently running against different version.
- jfp skills: advisory version-string pin; local content hash captured where possible.

UPGRADES (§10.2)
==================
'Update / verify skills' Diagnostics repair re-runs the active installer; verifies SHA-256s; reports drift; one-click upgrade pinned versions (audited config change).

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §1.1 reference table for skill installation.
- §8.1 — four-layer separation.
- §10.2 — repair action.
- §10.3 — retention + lock-file design.
- §17 jsm + jfp references.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- Loader resolves skills via lock file → jsm → jfp.
- Digest mismatch blocks affected jobs.
- Diagnostics 'Update / verify skills' repair works.
- Lock file changes audited.
- Test: simulate skill drift → affected job blocks; floating-skill (dev) project warns.

title: Phase 3: Daemon upgrade flow with verified provenance + rollback
description:
WHAT
====
The /v1/bootstrap/daemon/upgrade flow: download new binary via signed release URL, verify checksum + signature + provenance attestation, stop service, backup config + DB, install new binary, start, verify /v1/version, run compatibility checks. If migration fails, roll back to previous binary + DB backup. Desktop refuses write actions during migration; shows read-only diagnostics until compatibility is restored.

WHY (§11 'Daemon distribution', §11 'Tool version pinning')
==============================================================
Single static Go binary lifecycle: deterministic upgrades, predictable rollbacks, audited provenance. Bootstrap refuses install when provenance verification fails unless the user explicitly enables an insecure development override (audited, visibly marked in Diagnostics).

UPGRADE FLOW
=============
1. Desktop checks daemon /v1/version + capability registry; if newer binary exists at the signed-release URL pinned in Hoopoe's release manifest:
2. Show upgrade dialog with release notes, version diff, min-compatible-desktop check.
3. User approves; daemon downloads new binary to ~/.hoopoe/binaries/staging/.
4. Verify: SHA-256 checksum, OpenPGP signature, in-toto provenance attestation, SBOM check.
5. systemctl stop hoopoe; backup ~/.hoopoe/daemon.db + config; install new binary; start.
6. Wait for sd_notify ready; verify /v1/version; run compatibility checks (§10.3 migrations).
7. If anything fails: rollback to previous binary + DB backup; surface failure in Diagnostics.

INSECURE DEV OVERRIDE
======================
- Single config flag + matching env var.
- Audit entry with red 'INSECURE' label.
- Diagnostics shows persistent banner.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §11 — daemon distribution + version pinning.
- §10.3 — schema migrations + retention.
- §10.2 — Diagnostics 'Restart daemon' repair.
- §6.5 — systemd unit hardening.

CONSIDERATIONS
==============
- DB backup is an essential precondition for rollback; if backup fails, abort upgrade.
- Min-compatible-desktop check: if the new daemon is incompatible with the current desktop, surface 'desktop upgrade required' rather than installing.
- Upgrades run during quiet windows when possible; if a swarm is running, default to 'wait' rather than interrupt unless user forces.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- /v1/bootstrap/daemon/upgrade endpoint implemented.
- Provenance verification covers: checksum, signature, in-toto attestation, SBOM policy.
- Rollback tested: artificially fail post-install /v1/version → verify rollback restores previous state.
- Insecure-dev override tested + audited.
- Desktop refuses write actions while upgrade is in progress.

title: Phase 1 week 2: Lifted-code Codex-shape scrub — lint rule + manual checklist refusing 'thread/provider/chat/message-list' identifiers in non-vendored code (§14 risk)
description:
WHAT
====
§14 names 'Lifted code carries Codex-shaped assumptions' as a real risk: the t3code desktop layer was written for a chat/agent product, not a staged-cockpit product. Subtle assumptions (thread-centric data shapes, 'provider' abstractions, message-list virtualization) may leak through scrubbing. This bead is the cleanup task that prevents Codex-shape leakage into Hoopoe's purpose-built code.

§14 mitigation (verbatim): 'scrub aggressively in Phase 1, write integration tests against Hoopoe's own flows immediately, refactor anything that still says thread/provider/chat after week 2.'

WHEN
=====
End of Phase 1 week 2 (per §16 Phase 1 timeline). After the t3code lift is settled and the desktop shell navigates stages, but BEFORE Phase 2 daemon work depends on the lifted code shape.

LINT RULE (the durable enforcement)
=====================================
ESLint custom rule `hoopoe/no-codex-leakage`:
- Scope: `apps/desktop/src/**` EXCLUDING `apps/desktop/src/vendored/t3code/**`.
- Forbidden identifiers: `thread`, `Thread`, `provider`, `Provider`, `chat`, `Chat`, `message` (when used in list/timeline context — heuristic: 'messageList', 'messages.length' patterns), `MAX_THREAD_MESSAGES`, `ConversationItem`, `ChatTurn`.
- Forbidden imports from vendored/t3code: anything except the 5 explicit shape-adoptions in Appendix B (Patterns lifted: auth, settings, keybindings, sequence-cursor, atomic write).
- Allowed in: Activity-panel where 'message' refers to Agent Mail (clearly scoped); orchestrator-chat where 'chat' is the literal product feature.
- ESLint error (not warning) — fails CI.

MANUAL SCRUB CHECKLIST (the one-time pass)
=============================================
docs/source-provenance.md gets a checklist run at end of week 2:

```markdown
## Codex-shape scrub (Phase 1 week 2)

- [ ] grep 'thread' apps/desktop/src/ (excluding vendored) → renamed to bead/swarm/activity
- [ ] grep 'provider' apps/desktop/src/ (excluding vendored) → renamed; in CAAM context use 'account-provider' explicitly
- [ ] grep 'chat' apps/desktop/src/ (excluding vendored) → only orchestrator-chat allowed
- [ ] grep 'MAX_' apps/desktop/src/ (excluding vendored) → silent caps replaced with virtualization or 'showing latest N' (anti-pattern #3, see hp-iswv)
- [ ] grep 'Conversation', 'ChatTurn', 'MessageList' → renamed to ActivityTimeline / BeadList / AgentGrid
- [ ] Import audit: every `import from '../vendored/t3code/...'` is on the Appendix B 'Patterns lifted' approved list
- [ ] Storybook stories use Hoopoe domain language (bead, swarm, agent, plan), not chat/thread/conversation
- [ ] Component names match design system (StageHeader, BeadCard, AgentTile, etc.) NOT t3code names

After completion, sign + date the checklist; future PR reviews reference it.
```

INTEGRATION TESTS AGAINST HOOPOE'S OWN FLOWS
==============================================
§14 mitigation says 'write integration tests against Hoopoe's own flows immediately.' Concretely:
- Mock-bead-changed event flows from daemon → desktop renders the BeadCard correctly (NOT a chat-bubble).
- Mock-agent-state-changed event → AgentTile updates (NOT a Conversation-style message).
- Mock-mail event → ActivityTimeline appends (virtualized).
- Mock-approval event → ApprovalDialog opens (NOT a chat-style message).

These tests exist in hp-tg0 (smoke test suite) but this bead adds the explicit assertion that domain-language renders, not chat-shaped UI.

UNIT TESTS
============
- ESLint rule unit tests: positive (forbidden identifier triggers error), negative (allowed contexts pass).
- Vendored-import allowlist: tests that the 5 approved patterns can be imported, all others fail.

E2E TESTS
===========
- Run the scrub checklist as a CI script: `npm run scrub-check` greps the codebase and emits pass/fail per item.
- Stub a regression: rename a Hoopoe component back to 'ChatTurn' → CI fails loudly with the line number.

LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
======================
- Lint rule violations log: { rule, file, line, identifier, suggestedReplacement }.
- Manual scrub script logs each greppable item's match count.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §14 — risk: 'Lifted code carries Codex-shaped assumptions.'
- §16 Phase 1 week 2 — when this happens.
- Appendix B — t3code lift inventory (vendoring layout).
- hp-iswv — anti-pattern compliance audit (overlaps on anti-pattern #3 silent message caps).
- hp-c2ox risk #12 — risk-acceptance evidence references this bead.
- hp-zir — decompose main.ts (must be done before this scrub so renamed file boundaries are settled).
- hp-tg0 — Phase 1 smoke test suite (extended with domain-language assertions).

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- ESLint rule `hoopoe/no-codex-leakage` shipped + green in CI.
- docs/source-provenance.md has signed/dated scrub checklist.
- Integration tests assert domain-language UI rendering.
- Stub regression test demonstrates lint catches Codex-shape re-introduction.
- Phase 1 exit gate verifies scrub is complete (referenced from hp-dli).
close_reason: Lint rule scripts/codex-shape-scrub/check-codex-shape-scrub.ts (parallel to hp-ara provider-SDK lint and hp-rflj renderer-isolation lint) refuses Thread/Chat/Provider/MessageList/Conversation/ConversationItem/ChatTurn/MAX_THREAD_MESSAGES/messageList/threadList anywhere outside apps/desktop/src/vendored/t3code/. Comment+string literals stripped before matching; '// codex-shape-scrub-ok: <reason>' suppression supported. Wired into root bun run lint + bun run test (lint:codex-shape-scrub + test:codex-shape-scrub); 8 unit tests pass; full workspace scan reports 0 violations. Manual scrub checklist added to docs/source-provenance.md and signed. apps/desktop/.eslintrc.cjs documents the equivalent eslint id-denylist policy

title: Phase 2.5: Capability registry + degraded-mode contract wiring (§2.8)
description:
WHAT
====
Wire the capability registry epic into the daemon: /v1/capabilities returns a real registry, /v1/compatibility composes it with versions + min-desktop + event schema versions + migration state. Adapter contract tests assert capabilities (not just parser success).

WHY THIS MATTERS HERE (Phase 2.5 1a)
=====================================
Per §16: '/v1/capabilities and /v1/compatibility wired; ALL stage routes to capability-gated feature flags before building the feature UI.' Doing this once at Phase 2.5 means feature work in Phases 3+ can use capability flags directly without retrofit.

REQUIRED CAPABILITY IDS (illustrative, full list in capability registry epic)
==============================================================================
- ntm.sessions.list, ntm.panes.stream, ntm.approvals.list, ntm.robot.snapshot, ntm.swarm.halt
- br.issues.read, br.issues.update, br.dep.add
- bv.robot.triage, bv.robot.plan, bv.robot.insights, bv.robot.diff
- agent_mail.messages.read|send, agent_mail.reservations.list|release
- git.status.read, git.diff.read, git.push, git.unpushed.list
- caam.accounts.list, caam.account.switch
- caut.usage.snapshot
- dcg.verdicts.subscribe
- casr.session.resume
- pt.kill, srp.signals.read, sbh.cleanup
- ubs.scan
- jsm.skill.install|verify, jfp.skill.install
- oracle.serve.status, oracle.browser.run
- health.coverage, health.complexity, health.churn

DEGRADED-MODE BEHAVIOR
=======================
For each adapter and tending job:
- if_missing_required: block_job | run_read_only | emit_diagnostic
- if_missing_optional: continue_with_warning | suppress_related_detections
- activity_behavior: silent | diagnostics_only | activity_panel_warning

UI BEHAVIOR
===========
The desktop renders one of: available | degraded | unavailable | blocked-by-policy. Diagnostics explains the exact missing capability + fallback in use.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- capability registry epic.
- §2.8 — full registry shape.
- §10.2 — Diagnostics 'Update / verify skills' repair updates capabilities.
- §18.3 — adapter contract tests assert capabilities.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- /v1/capabilities returns a real registry from probed adapters.
- /v1/compatibility composes capabilities + versions.
- Every stage route in the renderer reads capability flags from /v1/capabilities and renders one of available/degraded/unavailable/blocked-by-policy states.
- Adapter contract tests: each adapter's capability assertions tested against fixtures (normal, missing-tool, unsupported-version, malformed, timeout, high-volume).
- Diagnostics page renders capability registry with per-tool status + fallback explanation.

title: Phase 4: Cockpit top bar — wire all chrome elements (project, tool health, swarm, beads, code-health, subscription, Activity)
description:
WHAT
====
Wire the always-visible cockpit chrome (§7.6) — project / repo / branch + clean/dirty Git state from local clone + 'N unpushed commits' from daemon; tool health dots; swarm state count; beads pulse; minimal seed code-health pill (Phase 11 lights it up fully); subscription-usage pill (Phase 8 wires caut + CAAM fully); Activity panel toggle with unread count.

WHY (§7.6)
===========
'The user must never have to dig to know whether code health is improving or degrading.' Top bar is the always-on dashboard answering 'what is the project's current state?' at a glance.

ELEMENTS (§7.6 left-to-right)
==============================
1. **Project / repo / branch** — clickable to switch project; shows clean/dirty Git state; warns on detached HEAD or unpushed commits.
2. **Tool health** — green/yellow/red dots for VPS daemon, NTM, Agent Mail, br/bv versions; click → Diagnostics.
3. **Swarm state** — count of running agents, idle agents, wedged/rate-limited agents; click → Swarm stage.
4. **Beads pulse** — ready / in-progress / blocked counts; click → Beads stage.
5. **Code-health pill** — avg coverage %, avg complexity, hotspot count, last-snapshot age. Color-coded against project thresholds. Click → Debugging/Hardening Health tab.
6. **Subscription-usage / rate-limit indicator** — per-provider quota usage from caut; rate-limit signals from caut/CAAM/CLI/NTM/rano. (Phase 8 + 10 wire fully; Phase 4 shows seed minimal data.)
7. **Activity panel toggle** — opens cross-stage Activity drawer; badged with unread count.

UPDATE CADENCE (§7.6)
======================
- Top-bar values come from the daemon's reconciled cache (§2.2).
- Pushed over the WebSocket event stream — NO polling from the renderer.
- Health pill updates within seconds of a new snapshot landing.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §7.6 — chrome spec.
- §10.5 — display latency p95 < 1s.
- design/mockups/v1/* — visual reference.

CONSIDERATIONS
==============
- Empty / loading / error states for every pill.
- 'Click → Diagnostics' / 'Click → Stage' interactions wired.
- Detached HEAD / unpushed-commit warnings are non-blocking but visible.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- All seven elements render with seed data in Phase 4.
- WebSocket-driven updates work; polling not used.
- Click-throughs to Diagnostics / Swarm stage / Beads stage / Health tab work.
- Storybook stories for each pill.
- Phase 4 acceptance: a VPS commit appears in the top bar 'N unpushed commits' indicator within seconds.

title: Worktree-cleanup tending task — GC stale ~/.hoopoe/work/ directories (§10.5 release-smoke gate)
description:
WHAT
====
Tending task that periodically GCs stale Git worktrees under ~/.hoopoe/work/<project-id>/health/<run-id>/ + ~/.hoopoe/work/<project-id>/review/<run-id>/. Without this, every health snapshot + review-round leaves a worktree behind; over weeks the cache grows multi-GB.

WHY
===
§10.5 release smoke check: 'health worktree cleanup leaves no stale multi-GB directories.' This is currently implicit; making it explicit as a tending task means it runs without manual intervention + is verifiable in CI.

JOB SHAPE (per §8.2)
======================
id: worktree-cleanup
schedule: every 1h
kind: deterministic
project_scope: null (global)
enabled_toolsets: [git_write]
script: worktree-cleanup.go
  - lists all ~/.hoopoe/work/*/health/* + ~/.hoopoe/work/*/review/* directories.
  - removes worktrees older than retention window (default: health 3 days, review 7 days).
  - uses git worktree remove --force where possible; falls back to rm -rf with audit on stale .git/worktrees pointer.
  - emits worktree_cleaned events with counts + freed bytes.
  - returns wakeAgent: false (mechanical task).
deliver: hoopoe_activity_panel
audit_always: true

CONFIGURATION
==============
- Retention windows configurable per-project under .hoopoe/tending/projects/<id>/jobs.d/worktree-cleanup.yaml.
- Global default in ~/.hoopoe/tending/global/jobs.d/worktree-cleanup.yaml.
- Setting changes audited.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §7.4.1 — health worktree isolation.
- §8.4 — initial tending job set (this task should be added to the canonical default set).
- §10.5 — release smoke check.
- hp-fb0 — initial tending job set (parent).
- hp-3at — health snapshot persistence (creates the worktrees this task cleans up).

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- Job committed under ~/.hoopoe/tending/global/jobs.d/worktree-cleanup.yaml as default-on.
- Pre-script implemented; deterministic (no LLM wake).
- Test: simulate 100 health snapshots over fixture time → cleanup runs → 0 stale dirs > retention window.
- Edge case: simulate failed worktree-remove (e.g., busy file) → stale .git/worktrees pointer pruned + audit entry; retry on next tick.
- Job appears in Phase 10's initial job set documentation (§8.4).

title: Phase 7: Kanban / DAG / Force views + bead drawer + dependency editing
description:
GOAL (§7.2 'Views', §12 Phase 7)
==================================
Implement the Beads stage views: Kanban (execution state) + DAG (dependency structure, top-down topological by longest-path-from-root, sibling order minimizing edge crossings) + Force (cluster/hotspot exploration, deferred per §13). Bead detail drawer covers overview, full context, dependencies, plan traceability, mail thread, files/reservations, tests/health, commits, review findings, audit history. Filters; dependency editing; cycle warnings; critical path + ready frontier; bv --robot-triage panel.

WHY (§7.2 'Views')
====================
Visualizing the bead graph is what lets the user CURATE confidently. Kanban shows execution flow; DAG shows structure; bead drawer is the bead's world.

VIEW SPECS
===========
- **Kanban** — columns by status (ready / claimed / in_progress / in_review / closed / blocked); cards = BeadCard component with status pill + priority chip + owner agent + files-touched count + age.
- **DAG** — top-down topological by longest-path-from-root (per design/mockups/v1/beads.jsx DAGView). Sibling order within a layer follows average parent x-coordinate to minimize edge crossings. React Flow primary; Cytoscape only if needed (§3).
- **Force** — DEFERRED per §13.

BEAD DETAIL DRAWER (§7.2)
===========================
- Overview (title, ID, status, priority, owner)
- Full context (description, acceptance criteria)
- Dependencies (incoming + outgoing)
- Plan traceability (which plan sections this implements)
- Mail thread (Agent Mail thread, bead-ID-keyed)
- Files / reservations (files-touched, current reservations)
- Tests / health (last build/test result, current coverage/complexity for files-touched)
- Commits (per the implementation-evidence ledger)
- Review findings
- Audit history

EXIT (§12 Phase 7)
====================
User can curate beads visually and understand graph state without a terminal.

ACCEPTANCE TEST (§18.1 Phase 7)
================================
DAG renders a fixture graph, highlights critical path and ready frontier, and remains usable at 500 visible nodes through clustering / virtualization.

title: SLO assertion library + packages/slo-targets.yaml (single source of truth for §10.5 numbers)
description:
WHAT
====
A small assertion library and a single canonical YAML file declaring every §10.5 SLO target by ID. Tests import the helpers and assert observed values; the Diagnostics screen reads the same YAML to display targets in production. The plan and the runtime never disagree about what the target is.

PACKAGES/SLO-TARGETS.YAML SHAPE
================================
targets:
  - id: desktop.reconnect.p95
    description: Desktop reconnect after laptop wake, p95 after network returns
    target: {percentile: 95, value: 10s, direction: max}
    source_section: §10.5
    enforced_in: [phase2.e2e, chaos]
  - id: events.replay.10min.p95
    description: Event replay after 10-minute disconnect, p95 for normal project channels
    target: {percentile: 95, value: 5s, direction: max}
    source_section: §10.5
    enforced_in: [phase2.e2e]
  - id: activity.event.display.p95
    description: Activity event display latency (excluding terminal chunks), p95
    target: {percentile: 95, value: 1s, direction: max}
    source_section: §10.5
    enforced_in: [phase9.e2e]
  - id: terminal.attach.first-byte.p95
    description: Terminal stream attach — latest ring visible
    target: {percentile: 95, value: 2s, direction: max}
    source_section: §10.5
    enforced_in: [phase8.e2e]
  - id: file.open.cached.p95
    description: Local file open from clone, cached blobs
    target: {percentile: 95, value: 150ms, direction: max}
    source_section: §10.5
    enforced_in: [phase4.e2e]
  - id: bead.kanban.load.1k.p95
    description: Bead Kanban load with 1,000 beads
    target: {percentile: 95, value: 1s, direction: max}
    source_section: §10.5
    enforced_in: [phase7.e2e]
  - id: dag.usable.500-nodes
    description: DAG usable at 500 visible nodes (clustered beyond)
    target: {boolean: true}
    source_section: §10.5
    enforced_in: [phase7.e2e]
  - id: job.cancellation.no-orphans
    description: No orphan child process groups after cancel test
    target: {boolean: true}
    source_section: §10.5
    enforced_in: [phase2.e2e, chaos]

ASSERTION API
==============
expectSloPass('desktop.reconnect.p95', observedSampleArray)
expectSlo('bead.kanban.load.1k.p95', observedSampleArray, {samples: 100})
expectSloBoolean('dag.usable.500-nodes', actualBool)

The library reads the YAML at test boot, computes the percentile from the observed samples, and either returns ok or fails with the gap.

DIAGNOSTICS INTEGRATION
=========================
The Diagnostics SLO screen renders one row per target, reading the same YAML, with current observed (rolling p95 from production telemetry where available) vs target. When a target is in violation in production, the row turns red and links to the Activity panel for investigation.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §10.5 — SLO targets list (the source of every entry in the YAML).
- §18 — verification matrix (each milestone test cites its SLO IDs).
- hp-75u — SLO metrics scaffolding (this bead is the data layer; hp-75u was the metrics collection).
- hp-qrk — parent epic.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- packages/slo-targets.yaml committed with all §10.5 entries + their owning phase.
- @hoopoe/slo TS package + internal/slo Go package with the assertion API.
- Diagnostics SLO screen reads the same YAML.
- Lint rule: any new SLO mention in code must reference an ID in the YAML.

title: Phase 10: Tending scheduler infrastructure (cron + interval + event + on-demand; lease-based; durable; restartable)
description:
WHAT
====
Layer 1 of §8.1: Go scheduler for tending jobs. Cron + interval + event triggers + on-demand. Lease-based, durable, restartable. Fires per-job pre-script on schedule; writes audit entry on every tick regardless of outcome (§8.3.2).

JOB DEFINITIONS LAYOUT (§8.2)
================================
Editable definition (YAML/JSON):
- ~/.hoopoe/tending/global/jobs.d/*.yaml
- ~/.hoopoe/tending/projects/<project-id>/jobs.d/*.yaml

User-editable, atomic write (tempfile + rename + fsync), watched for hot reload. Source for definitions only.

Runtime state in daemon SQLite:
- imported job revision
- next-run time
- active leases
- run attempts
- trigger dedupe
- retry counters
- cooldowns
- dead-letter state
- last decision payload
- scheduler metrics

DEFINITION SHAPE (§8.2 full schema; the long YAML in §8.2)
============================================================
id, name, kind (deterministic | gated_agent | orchestrator_chat | external_webhook), version, revision, schedule (cron/interval/event/on demand), project_scope, enabled_toolsets, capabilities_required, capabilities_optional, degraded_mode, script, skills, skill_requirements, prompt, context_policy, deliver, repeat, paused, timeout, max_concurrency, misfire_policy, retry_policy, dead_letter_after, audit_always.

CORRECTNESS INVARIANTS (§8.3.2)
=================================
- A recurring job NEVER marked completed unless repeat count exhausted or explicitly disabled.
- Missing schedule deps / malformed cron / failed next-run → status: error, NOT completed.
- Each job execution holds a lease. Daemon crash → later daemon reclaims expired leases + marks interrupted run.
- Default max_concurrency: 1.
- Every run has timeout + cancellation path.
- Missed runs after daemon downtime follow misfire_policy.
- Repeated failures move to dead-letter (visible in Diagnostics).
- Deterministic jobs do NOT start LLM session — pre-script logic only; emit events only when action taken or failure.
- Due jobs selected in ONE transaction; each due run receives durable run record before execution.
- Tick loop dispatches due jobs through bounded worker pool; one long run cannot block unrelated due jobs.
- Every due job resolves to one of: started / skipped_by_policy / skipped_by_misfire_policy / paused / leased_elsewhere / dead_lettered / scheduler_error.
- 'Skipped' is an explicit audited state.
- Scheduler metrics: due runs, started runs, skipped runs, misfires, lease steals, dead letters, average tick duration, longest queued delay.

CLI (§8.2)
============
hoopoe tending {list,create,edit,pause,resume,run,remove,status,tick} — same shape as Hermes.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §8 — full architecture.
- §17 Hermes Agent reference.
- §1.8 — why skill-attached jobs over Go state machine.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- Scheduler runs cron + interval + event + on-demand triggers.
- Lease reclamation on crash tested.
- Misfire policies tested.
- Dead-letter state reachable + visible in Diagnostics.
- All correctness invariants satisfied + tested.
- CLI works.
- Definitions hot-reload.

title: AGENTS.md for the Hoopoe project itself (so agents working on Hoopoe code follow the same contract)
description:
WHAT
====
AGENTS.md authored for the Hoopoe project itself (the meta-case): the operating contract that agents working on Hoopoe code follow when Hoopoe's own development uses ACFS swarms. This is distinct from hp-dtx (per-project AGENTS.md surfacing) — that is the user-facing feature; this bead is Hoopoe's dogfood.

WHY
===
Hoopoe's whole product position is 'the cockpit for users running ACFS swarms.' Building Hoopoe with the same toolchain (br + bv + ntm + Agent Mail + caam + rch + UBS + jsm + jfp) is the most credible test of the product, and the dogfood lap surfaces UX and operating-contract gaps faster than any user study.

This bead is the AGENTS.md file at the repo root + the /docs/development/agent-operating-contract.md companion.

CONTENT (must include)
========================
1. **Project overview** — what Hoopoe is, who it's for, the four stages, the Activity panel, the source-of-truth principle (§1.1), the no-terminal default UX (§7.3 / Appendix C #12).
2. **Repository layout** — apps/{desktop, daemon}, packages/{schemas, design-system}, design/mockups/, docs/, .beads/, scripts/.
3. **Toolchain in use** — br for issues, bv for triage, ntm for swarm, Agent Mail for coordination, jsm for skills (jfp fallback), rch for builds, UBS for review.
4. **Build/test commands** — bun run dev / bun run typecheck / bun run lint / bun run test / cd apps/daemon && go build / go test ./... / playwright install + bun run e2e.
5. **Plan + beads workflow** — plan.md is the source for high-level decisions; .beads/issues.jsonl is the canonical bead state; br sync --flush-only after edits + git add + commit.
6. **Coding conventions** — TypeScript strict, no any, no console.log (use @hoopoe/logger), Go gofmt + golangci-lint, no provider-SDK imports (Appendix C #11), no PubSub.unbounded (Appendix B #1), no terminal in default UI components (Appendix C #12).
7. **Commit + branch policy** — feature branches; bead ID in commit messages; auto-push within 5 min (§7.3 push policy applies even to Hoopoe's own swarms).
8. **Approvals** — destructive ops (force-push, dropping migrations, rotating signing secrets) go through the unified approvals queue; SLB optional for autopilot mode.
9. **Skills loaded by default** — vibing-with-ntm + ntm + beads-workflow; pinned in .hoopoe/skills.lock.json.
10. **Per-bead checklist** — claim → reserve → implement → tests → self-review → close → release reservation → push.
11. **DCG verdicts** — agents respect DCG; Hoopoe ingests verdicts into the approvals queue.
12. **No bare bv** — robot surfaces only.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §7.3 kickoff prompt — rules baseline.
- §13 Must include — AGENTS.md detection/editor link is part of the project import.
- hp-dtx — user-project AGENTS.md surfacing.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- AGENTS.md committed at repo root.
- /docs/development/agent-operating-contract.md companion with longer-form rationale.
- Smoke test asserts AGENTS.md is detected by Hoopoe's own importer.
- A Hoopoe development swarm runs successfully against the operating contract for at least one closed bead.
close_reason: Added Hoopoe agent operating contract companion doc

title: pt / srp / sbh adapters (deterministic actuators for watch-safety-thresholds)
description:
WHAT
====
Adapters for the three deterministic actuators behind watch-safety-thresholds (§8.4):
- **pt** (process-terminator) — kills genuinely wedged processes with audit.
- **srp** (System Resource Protection — ananicy-cpp + monitor) — disk/CPU/load signals.
- **sbh** (disk-pressure defense) — stale-artifact cleanup.

WHY (§8.4 'CROSSING A HARD THRESHOLD emits typed deterministic action intents')
=================================================================================
- budget breach → swarm.halt (NTM)
- disk pressure → sbh-driven cleanup of stale artifacts
- genuinely wedged process (no output + no syscalls + over deterministic threshold) → pt kill_wedged_process

These actuators are deterministic — no LLM judgment. Pre-script (§8.3) emits typed action intents executed by the same action executor as agent ActionPlans (§8.3.1).

CAPABILITY IDS (§2.8)
======================
- pt.kill
- srp.signals.read
- sbh.cleanup

ACTION INTEGRATION (§8.3.1)
==============================
- pt → agent.kill_wedged_process action with postcondition 'NTM/tmux no longer reports the process'.
- sbh → no formal ActionPlan type because cleanup runs synchronously in the pre-script with audit; treated as a one-time action.
- srp → signals only; not an actuator (read-only).

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §1.1 reference table — these adapters.
- §8.4 watch-safety-thresholds.
- §8.5 stale-artifact cleanup.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- All three adapters wrapped + tested.
- Capability registry reports correctly.
- watch-safety-thresholds pre-script uses them.
- Adapter contract tests pass.

title: Diagnostics screen complete repair-action catalog (per §10.2 table)
description:
WHAT
====
Implement every repair action in the §10.2 Diagnostics table. Each is backed by a typed daemon RPC and audited.

REPAIR ACTIONS (§10.2)
========================
| Repair | Safety behavior |
|--------|-----------------|
| Restart daemon | Confirmation; streams systemd result; preserves job registry where possible. |
| Restart NTM / Agent Mail service | Project/session impact warning. |
| Re-run ACFS doctor | Read-only unless user approves fixes. |
| Clear desktop local clone | Deletes mirror only; never touches VPS or origin. |
| Detect / archive orphan project clones | ru prune; --archive non-destructive default; --delete requires confirmation; no-op when no orphans. |
| Force release stale reservation | Requires reason; posts Agent Mail notice; audit links to stale evidence. |
| Replay events from sequence | Read-only for UI gaps. |
| Rebuild bead read model | Re-reads br/.beads; cache-only. |
| Re-run health snapshot | In isolated worktree; queues behind active health jobs. |
| Update / verify skills (jsm or jfp) | Re-runs active installer; verifies SHA-256s; reports drift; one-click upgrade pinned versions. |
| Show raw pane (per agent) | Streams agent's tmux pane output to Diagnostics-only TerminalPane; opt-in per agent; audited on toggle on/off; auto-closes after configurable idle window; read-only. |
| Restart Oracle (oracle serve on Mac) | Tears down + re-launches local oracle serve; pauses in-flight Pro plan rounds; does not affect VPS-side CLIs. |

EACH REPAIR
=============
- Typed daemon RPC.
- Audit entry with actor + reason + result.
- Confirmation dialog where destructive.
- Approval gate where required (force-release, raise-budget-cap, etc.).
- Impact warning where applicable.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §10.2 — full table.
- §5.3 — destructive operations require approval.
- §10.3 — schema migrations + rollback (used by daemon restart).

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- All 12 repair actions implemented.
- Each has confirmation / approval / audit appropriately.
- Diagnostics screen UI (Phase 13 final polish task) renders every repair.
- E2E tests for at least force-release-stale-reservation, restart-daemon, re-run-acfs-doctor.

title: rano adapter (per-call latency / error signals; diagnostics-only for v1)
description:
WHAT
====
rano (network observer for AI CLIs) adapter — diagnostics-only signal source for v1. Provides per-call latency + per-call error signals from observed network traffic between AI CLIs (Claude Code / Codex / Gemini) and their backend providers, without intercepting traffic itself.

WHY
===
caut tracks subscription-quota usage at the per-window level (e.g., '42% of daily Claude Max'); rano's per-call signal is finer-grained — useful when an agent rate-limits and we want to see whether the provider is returning 429s, slow responses, or partial failures. In v1 this surfaces in Diagnostics only — not as a top-bar pill or a tend-swarm input — to avoid premature reliance on a tool that's still maturing. Post-MVP the signal can promote into the recovery actions surface (§7.3) and tend-swarm detections (§8.4).

SCOPE (v1)
============
- Diagnostics screen 'Recent AI CLI calls' table: timestamp, harness, model, latency p50/p95, error count, last error class.
- Capability rano.signals.read.
- Adapter writes parsed observations into the daemon read-model cache; cache reconciles per §2.2.
- NOT wired into top-bar usage pill (caut is the source of truth there).
- NOT wired into watch-safety-thresholds (caut + CLI status messages + NTM events are sufficient v1; rano can be a refinement post-MVP).

CONFIGURATION
==============
- Per-project config; respects model-context policy (rano observations may include redacted endpoints, never raw bodies).

OBSERVABILITY
==============
- Adapter run logs: window observed, total calls, errors, p50/p95 latencies.
- Failures to parse observations log the offending bytes (redacted) for debugging.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §7.3 — recovery actions (post-MVP rano integration).
- §8.4 watch-safety-thresholds — capabilities_optional includes rano signals.
- §13 — 'Can defer' rano per-call latency dashboards.
- §14 — subscription rate-limit risk mitigation.
- §17 — rano reference.
- hp-r33 — capability registry.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- Adapter implemented + capability rano.signals.read reported.
- Diagnostics 'Recent AI CLI calls' table renders rano observations.
- Adapter contract tests (hp-8a8): happy / missing / unsupported version / malformed output / timeout / high-volume.
- Documented in docs/integration-contracts/rano.md.
- v1: explicitly NOT wired into top-bar pill or tend-swarm — wiring deferred to post-MVP.

title: Phase 10: Tending scheduler + initial job set + skill loader (jsm preferred / jfp fallback)
description:
GOAL (§8, §12 Phase 10)
=========================
The four-layer tending architecture (§8.1). Layer 1 scheduler (Go), Layer 2 per-job pre-scripts (deterministic Go), Layer 3 agent runtime (skills loaded via jsm/jfp), Layer 4 skills as content. The wakeAgent / [SILENT] cost-and-noise floor. Initial job set: tend-swarm, watch-safety-thresholds, push-stale-commits, snapshot-health, drift-check, review-readiness-check, orchestrator-chat. Approval-gate integration. Skill loader (jsm preferred, jfp fallback).

WHY (§1.8, §8.6, §8.7)
========================
'Tend agents with skill-attached jobs, not bespoke loops.' When vibing-with-ntm evolves, behavior evolves — no PR to write. Cost stays bounded (wakeAgent:false on every healthy tick). Panel stays quiet ([SILENT] on healthy agent runs). Deterministic safety where it matters (hard thresholds in pre-script). Orchestrator chat becomes literal (same agent runtime as scheduled jobs). One runtime. Auditability. User-editable.

EXIT (§12 Phase 10)
=====================
Hoopoe can tend a real swarm for an hour with visible, explainable, skill-driven interventions; healthy hour produces near-zero LLM cost (most ticks wakeAgent:false) + near-zero Activity-panel noise (most agent runs [SILENT]); stuck/drifting swarm triggers exactly the agent attention + panel output the situation warrants; user can chat with orchestrator-chat agent in Activity panel; orchestrator can read state, propose actions, execute via the same approval gates as user actions.

ACCEPTANCE TEST (§18.1 Phase 10)
==================================
Healthy swarm hour produces mostly wakeAgent:false / [SILENT]; stuck fixture triggers a skill-driven intervention and audit.

title: Phase 1 component subtask: CommandPalette ⌘K (registry-driven, fuzzy search, when-clause filtering)
description:
WHAT
====
CommandPalette (⌘K) — registry-driven, fuzzy-search, when-clause filtered command palette covering every reachable command in Hoopoe.

WHY
===
The plan's Appendix B anti-pattern #6 explicitly calls out t3code's implicit string-switch dispatch as a non-scaling approach: 'won't scale to per-agent commands.' Hoopoe must build a real command registry from day one. The palette is the user-visible front door of that registry; the keybindings system (hp-rth) is the keyboard front door of the same registry.

REGISTRY API
==============
commandRegistry.register(id, handler, {whenContextKeys, defaultKeybinding?, category, label, description?})
- id: stable string ID (e.g., 'project.import', 'swarm.broadcast', 'bead.toggleClaim').
- handler: typed function executed when the command fires.
- whenContextKeys: array of context keys the command requires; the palette filters out commands whose when-clause evaluates false in current context.
- defaultKeybinding: optional default key combo (overridable by user keybindings file).
- category: groups in the palette UI (Project / Plan / Beads / Swarm / Activity / Diagnostics / Help / Window).
- label: human-readable name.
- description: optional one-liner shown under label.

PALETTE BEHAVIOR
==================
- ⌘K from any stage opens palette as overlay.
- Fuzzy search across id + label + description; matches highlighted.
- when-clause filtering hides commands not applicable in current context (e.g., 'swarm.broadcast' hidden when no swarm is running).
- Recently-used commands surfaced at top (per-user preference, persisted in client-settings.json).
- Selection fires the handler via main-process IPC (renderer never runs a privileged command directly).
- Esc closes; Enter executes; arrow keys navigate.

CONTEXT KEY VALIDATION
========================
Per Appendix B anti-pattern #7: 'Unknown when-clause context keys evaluate to false.' Hoopoe validates context keys against a known set at parse time + fails loudly on typos. Same rule applies to palette and keybindings (one shared validator).

ACCESSIBILITY
===============
- Full keyboard navigation (no mouse required).
- Screen-reader announces matched commands.
- AA contrast for every state.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- Appendix B #6 (real command registry) and #7 (when-clause validation).
- §16 Phase 1 week 2 — command registry + ⌘K palette tasks.
- hp-rth — keybindings (shares registry).
- hp-i62 — reusable component set.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- Command registry API implemented + lint rule rejecting raw string-switch dispatch in renderer code.
- ⌘K palette renders against registry; fuzzy search + when-clause filtering correct.
- Context-key validator rejects unknown keys at parse time.
- Recently-used surface persists via client-settings.json.
- Storybook stories cover empty / typing / matches / no-matches / when-filtered states.
- Coverage ≥80% on registry + palette.
close_reason: Added CommandPalette primitive, fuzzy ranking, when-context filtering, stories, and tests

title: Phase 5 / §7.1: Mac power-assertions during active ChatGPT Pro Oracle rounds (NSProcessInfo + IOPMAssertion + caffeinate fallback) — prevent system/display sleep for the duration of the round
description:
WHAT
====
§7.1 explicitly mandates: 'a single ChatGPT Pro round needs the Mac awake and online for the duration of that round (extended-reasoning runs can be 5–30 minutes). Hoopoe surfaces this clearly in the round's progress UI so a user about to close the lid sees "this will pause the active ChatGPT Pro round."'

But the *prevention* mechanism — the actual power assertion that keeps the Mac awake — is not yet owned by any bead. This bead implements the active sleep-prevention machinery for the MVP Mac-side Oracle topology so a Pro round does not silently fail when the user steps away. The §7.1 trade-off is explicit: 'the rest of the planning pipeline is sleep-resilient on the VPS, but a single ChatGPT Pro round needs the Mac awake.' That makes preventing sleep a *correctness* concern for Pro rounds, not a UX nice-to-have.

WHY THIS IS A FOCUSED BEAD
==========================
- hp-nd42 covers macOS sleep/wake **fault-injection testing** (verifying recovery), not the prevention mechanism for Pro rounds.
- hp-s2cg covers ConnectionManager FSM transitions on sleep/wake (reconnect logic), not prevention.
- The Oracle adapter beads cover the IPC + browser orchestration but not the OS-level power assertion.
- Without active power assertion, the documented user-warning in §7.1 ('this will pause the active ChatGPT Pro round') is the user's *only* defense — a fragile UX baseline. The fix is to actively keep the Mac awake whenever a Pro round is in flight, then *also* show the warning so the user understands what's happening.

POWER-ASSERTION LADDER
======================
Hoopoe's main process holds power assertions ONLY while a Pro round is active. The ladder degrades gracefully:

1. **Primary: Electron PowerSaveBlocker (cross-platform)** — `powerSaveBlocker.start('prevent-display-sleep')` for short rounds (<5min) where the Mac sleeping during planning is the only concern; `'prevent-app-suspension'` for longer ones to prevent the renderer from being suspended while still allowing display sleep at the user's preference.
2. **Mac-native fallback: NSProcessInfo `beginActivity:withReason:`** — for rounds >5min, Hoopoe's main process calls this via a small Objective-C bridge in `apps/desktop/src/main/macPowerAssert.ts`. NSActivityOptions: `NSActivityIdleDisplaySleepDisabled | NSActivityIdleSystemSleepDisabled | NSActivityUserInitiated | NSActivityLatencyCritical` matches Apple's guidance for 'long-running user-initiated work.'
3. **Last-resort fallback: `/usr/bin/caffeinate -dimsu -w $ELECTRON_PID`** — spawned as a subprocess if both Electron and the NSProcessInfo bridge fail (e.g., older macOS, sandboxed builds). Tied to Electron's pid via `-w` so it dies if Electron does. Audited as a degraded-mode entry.

The OS-level assertions are **scoped to the Pro round lifecycle**: acquired when `oracle --engine browser` actually starts (not just queued), released on round exit (success / failure / cancel / Oracle restart). Held assertions persist across desktop window minimize/hide; released eagerly when round finishes. **Never** held during VPS-only CLI rounds (Claude / Codex / Gemini) — those don't need the Mac awake.

IDENTIFIER + UI SURFACES
========================
- New top-bar pill: 'Mac kept awake — Pro round in progress (5m elapsed / ~25m typical max).' Click → opens active round in Plan stage. Color: cream-on-yellow per design tokens.
- Dock-icon badge: small lightning glyph overlay while assertion held.
- Activity panel: 'Pro round started — Mac will stay awake until X completes' urgent informational entry.
- Settings (hp-wg5p) toggle: 'Disable power assertions (ChatGPT Pro rounds may pause if Mac sleeps).' Default off (assertions on).
- Diagnostics (hp-6d7): 'Active power assertions' status card — name, reason string, duration, owning Pro-round ID.

ASSERTION SAFETY
================
- Track every assertion with a typed handle in main process: `{ assertionId, level, reason, ownerRoundId, acquiredAt, releasedAt? }`.
- On main-process crash, OS releases native assertions; `caffeinate` subprocess dies with the parent thanks to `-w`. No leak.
- Watchdog: if a Pro round's daemon-job event stream goes silent for >180s and the assertion is still held, log a warning to the audit log and force-release after 600s of silence (the round is presumed dead). Surfaced as a Diagnostics 'recent problems' entry.
- Battery context: if user is unplugged AND battery <20%, downgrade to 'prevent-display-sleep' only (allow system sleep) and show a banner: 'Pro round running on battery — Mac may sleep when battery is critical.' Audited.
- Test: never held when no Pro round is active (assertion-leak guard runs at idle every 60s and raises an internal alert if a stale assertion is found).

UNIT TESTS (10) — `apps/desktop/src/main/__tests__/macPowerAssert.test.ts`
===========================================================================
1. `PowerAssertionManager.acquire()` returns a typed handle and records `acquiredAt` in audit-shaped log.
2. Acquire then release fires a single audit pair; a doubled release is a no-op (idempotent) with a warning logged.
3. Round-cancel path during `acquire()` (round dies in <100ms) releases assertion immediately and emits structured log `{ event: 'pro-round.power_release', reason: 'round_cancelled', round_id, held_ms }`.
4. Mock-Electron PowerSaveBlocker primary path returns success → no NSProcessInfo or caffeinate is spawned (assertion-ladder discipline asserted via spy counts).
5. Mock-Electron PowerSaveBlocker.start throws → fallback to NSProcessInfo bridge fires; structured log records `fallback_used: 'nsprocessinfo'`; if NSProcessInfo also throws → caffeinate spawns; structured log `fallback_used: 'caffeinate'`.
6. `caffeinate` subprocess dies when test parent process killed (verifies `-w $PID` semantics via real child-process spawn in test, not mock).
7. Watchdog: simulate Pro-round event-stream silence for >180s → warning written; >600s → `force_release` invoked; assertion released; audit + structured log captured.
8. Battery-low downgrade: stub `powerMonitor.onBatteryPower=true` + battery <20% → assertion downgrades to display-only AND user-visible banner event emitted on `activity.warning` topic.
9. Idle-leak guard: at process idle, if no Pro round is active and an assertion is held, internal alert is raised (no force-release in unit test — surfaces only) with structured log `alert: 'power_assertion_leak_detected'`.
10. Concurrent Pro rounds: two rounds (R1, R2) acquire; release of R1 keeps assertion held until R2 also releases (refcount-style); structured logs show `held_count` transitions 0→1→2→1→0.

E2E TESTS (7) — `apps/desktop/e2e/proRoundPowerMgmt.e2e.ts`
===============================================================
1. **Happy path on real macOS**: Start fixture Pro round → `pmset -g assertions` shows Hoopoe's `PreventUserIdleSystemSleep` line → round completes → `pmset` no longer shows it. Logs from main process at INFO: `pro-round.power_acquire` and `pro-round.power_release` with `held_ms` field.
2. **Assertion held across renderer minimize**: Start round → `Cmd+H` to hide app → confirm assertion still held via `pmset -g assertions` → unhide → assertion still held → finish round → released.
3. **Display-sleep + system-sleep prevention smoke**: Set system idle to 1min via fixture script → start 90s mock Pro round → after 70s confirm display + system are still awake (hint: `ioreg -l | grep IdleSleepPreventer` shows owner `Hoopoe`) → round ends → display dims within next idle threshold.
4. **Round failure releases assertion**: Spawn a Pro round that fails (Oracle crash fixture) → assertion released within 5s of failure event → `pmset` confirms.
5. **Battery-on degraded mode**: Run with stubbed `powerMonitor` (battery=true, level=15%) → start Pro round → only `PreventUserIdleDisplaySleep` appears in pmset, NOT system-sleep → in-app banner visible to Playwright with text 'Pro round running on battery'.
6. **Assertion leak detection on simulated crash**: Force-kill round-job process with `SIGKILL` while assertion held → watchdog detects silence >180s (test uses sped-up clock via dependency-injected Now) → audit entry written → assertion force-released after 600s simulated.
7. **Two concurrent Pro rounds refcount**: Start R1 → start R2 → terminate R1 → assertion still present → terminate R2 → released. e2e log captures the refcount transitions.

DETAILED STRUCTURED LOGGING (per hp-lxs)
==========================================
Every assertion lifecycle event emits a JSON line to the desktop structured log:
- `pro-round.power_acquire` { round_id, model_id (gpt-5.4-pro), oracle_topology (mac|vps), assertion_level, mechanism (powersaveblocker|nsprocessinfo|caffeinate), reason_string, battery: {power_source, level, on_battery}, mac_os_version, hoopoe_version }
- `pro-round.power_release` { round_id, held_ms, reason (round_complete|round_failed|round_cancelled|battery_downgrade|watchdog_force_release|user_disabled), mechanism, residual_handles_after_release }
- `pro-round.power_warning` { round_id, kind (silence_threshold_180s|battery_critical_15pct|leak_detected|fallback_used), context }
- `pro-round.power_audit` (mirrored to audit.jsonl per hp-g73) — same shape but with actor + correlationId

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §7.1 'Where planning runs' + 'Two execution modes': Mac-side Oracle topology requirement
- §13 'Can defer': VPS-resident Oracle (post-MVP), so power assertion is the v1 mitigation
- §10.2 Diagnostics 'Restart Oracle' repair: confirms Pro-round lifecycle has explicit failure paths (cookie expiry, Chrome crash, port conflict)
- §14 risk 'Laptop sleep breaks perception of reliability' + §10.5 SLO 'Desktop reconnect after laptop wake p95 under 10s': prevention-then-recovery is the strategy
- design tokens (cream/dark): top-bar pill style

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- `PowerAssertionManager` class with the 3-level ladder, refcounting, watchdog
- IPC handler `power.acquire` / `power.release` from renderer (called by Plan workspace when a Pro round starts / ends)
- Top-bar pill component renders when at least one assertion is held
- Activity panel emits the urgent informational entry on Pro-round start
- Settings toggle to disable assertions (default off)
- Diagnostics 'active assertions' status card
- All 10 unit tests + 7 e2e tests passing
- Structured logs reviewed manually on 1 real Pro round to confirm field coverage
- Plan documentation: 'Pro round expected to run unattended → Hoopoe will keep your Mac awake. Active battery-saver mode may downgrade prevention.'

title: Phase 1.5: Wire SettingsBridge changes into audit trail
description:
Root cause: review-findings.md lines 169-174 show SettingsBridge has audit primitives available but never calls SettingsAuditTrail/auditResolvedTreeDelta/auditSettingsChange. Security-relevant settings are persisted silently, so the renderer can label fields as audited while no setting_changed audit entries exist.

Acceptance criteria:
- SettingsBridge accepts an audit sink and actor context for user/global/project setting writes.
- Security-relevant setting deltas emit append-only setting_changed audit entries with before/after tier/source metadata and redaction applied.
- Non-security settings do not emit audit noise.
- Unit tests cover desktop.updateChannel or safety preset producing one audit row, daemon.logLevel producing none, and write failures preserving settings behavior while surfacing a diagnostic.
- Cross-link: review-findings.md "SettingsBridge ↔ SettingsAuditTrail — [HIGH] audit module exists but is not wired"; docs/post-phase1-reality-check.md §1 Phase 1 Week 2 notes review-found audit wiring gaps.
close_reason: shipped (audit wiring complete; UI-side hp-wg5p is the renderer surface, separate dependency direction)

title: Multi-VPS / Connection entity migration (post-MVP per ADR-0001)
description:
WHAT
====
Per §4.1 + ADR-0001: when multi-VPS becomes a real need, a Connection entity will own VPS state and projects will key to it. v1 has implicit single Connection with no UI / no schema presence. This bead documents the migration plan + scaffolds entry points.

WHY POST-MVP
==============
v1 is single-VPS-per-install (ADR-0001). Multi-VPS adds Connection entity, profile management, switching, per-VPS auth. v1 use case (solo builder + small teams) doesn't justify the added complexity.

MIGRATION PLAN
================
- Connection entity: {connectionId, host, port, username, status: VPS lifecycle, bearerKey, ssh-key-ref, lastConnectedAt}.
- Project schema gains connectionId foreign key.
- Schema migration walks existing single-Connection install + back-fills connectionId.
- UI gains Connection picker (possibly merged with Project picker).
- Auth model gains per-Connection bearers + per-Connection rotation.
- Top-bar shows Connection + Project rather than just Project.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §4.1 — VPS lifecycle vs project lifecycle.
- ADR-0001 — single VPS per install for v1.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- This bead reflects the migration plan.
- Code paths scaffolded so adding a Connection entity is mechanical.
- Schema future-proofed (no schema breaks expected).

title: Test runner config + JSON evidence emitter (vitest desktop + go test daemon + playwright e2e)
description:
WHAT
====
The concrete test runners and the reporters that turn test runs into reproducible evidence files. Three runners, one shape:

1. **Desktop unit + integration**: vitest with workers=auto, --reporter=verbose --reporter=json --reporter=html. JSON output goes to docs/test-evidence/<phase>/<timestamp>/desktop-<runId>.json with the structured-logging envelope embedded.
2. **Daemon unit + integration**: go test ./... with gotestsum --jsonfile + go-junit-report XML for IDE/CI integration. JSON output to docs/test-evidence/<phase>/<timestamp>/daemon-<runId>.json.
3. **App-level e2e**: playwright with electron launch fixture, traces always-on, screenshots on failure, video on failure. Output bundled into the same evidence dir.

REPORTERS
==========
A custom JSON evidence emitter on top of each runner that writes:
{
  runId, ts, gitSha, daemonVersion, fixtureScenario, runner, phase,
  results: [{name, file, status, durationMs, slo: {target, observed, passed}, logSlice: [<last 200 log entries>]}],
  coverage: {statements, branches, lines, functions, deltaVsMain},
  artifacts: [<paths>],
  redactionStats: {patternsMatched: {...}}
}

The evidence file IS the test log — there is no other 'logs you have to dig for' surface. CI uploads it as a build artifact + commits a slim summary into the PR comment.

SLO INTEGRATION
================
Test cases tagged @slo:<targetId> assert against the §10.5 numeric targets:
- desktop.reconnect.p95 ≤ 10s after network returns
- events.replay.10min.p95 ≤ 5s
- activity.event.display.p95 ≤ 1s
- terminal.attach.first-byte.p95 ≤ 2s
- file.open.cached.p95 ≤ 150ms
- bead.kanban.load.1k.p95 ≤ 1s
- dag.usable.500-nodes (boolean: usable = scrollable + < 500ms layout)
- job.cancellation.no-orphans (boolean)

The SLO assertion library reads the target from a single source-of-truth file packages/slo-targets.yaml so plan + tests + Diagnostics dashboards never disagree about what 'p95 ≤ 1s' means.

TEST CATEGORIES (lint-enforced)
==================================
@unit, @integration, @e2e, @chaos, @slo, @smoke, @release. CI selects per-PR vs nightly vs release-gate via tag filters.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §10.5 — SLO targets.
- §18.1 — milestone acceptance tests (each becomes an @e2e test in this framework).
- §18.4 — release smoke checks (an @release tag).
- hp-qrk — parent epic.
- hp-tg0 — Phase 1 smoke test suite consumes this framework.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- vitest + gotestsum + playwright wired with the JSON evidence emitter.
- packages/slo-targets.yaml is the single source for SLO numbers (Diagnostics dashboards consume the same file).
- @slo-tagged tests fail when the observed value crosses the target.
- Coverage delta enforcement runs per-PR and blocks regressions.
- docs/testing.md describes the runners, tags, and evidence layout.
