Tests for Phase 0's research-spike output: that the fixture corpus is correct, comprehensive, and deterministic. Phase 0 is upstream of every other phase test; if its fixtures are broken, every downstream test sits on rotten ground.

UNIT TESTS
============
- Each fixture under packages/fixtures/phase0-<date>/ validates against the schemas in packages/schemas/.
- For every adapter (br, bv, ntm, agent_mail, ru, git, caam, caut, dcg, casr, pt, srp, sbh, ubs, jsm, jfp, oracle, health_<lang>):
  • happy-path fixture exists.
  • missing-tool fixture exists.
  • unsupported-version fixture exists.
  • malformed-output fixture exists.
  • timeout fixture exists.
  • high-volume fixture exists.
- Snapshot script (hp-6v3): runs against a research-spike VPS + against Mock Flywheel; output validates against schema; idempotent (same input + same VPS state → byte-identical output modulo timestamps).
- Per-tool integration contract docs (hp-78m): each docs/integration-contracts/<tool>.md covers command surface, output shape, version pinning, fallback behavior, known gotchas.
- Manual-wizard-walkthrough fixtures (hp-jvm): per-step screenshots + JSON outputs + timing data committed; happy path + at least one failure path captured.

E2E TESTS
==========
1. Phase 0 acceptance: snapshot script run twice on same VPS → byte-identical output (Phase 0 exit criterion).
2. Mock Flywheel boot: load each fixture scenario, verify all expected events stream out, no parse errors.
3. Fixture-corpus completeness: every adapter has all 6 fixture states; CI fails if any is missing.
4. Per-tool contract drift detection: when a tool's actual output drifts from the committed fixture, the next snapshot script run fails CI with a clear diff; user updates the fixture deliberately.
5. ACFS bootstrap parser fixtures (hp-qq9): clean install / partially-complete install / failed-dependency install / already-installed-ACFS run — all four committed and parsed correctly.

LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
======================
- Every snapshot-script invocation logs the VPS IP + ACFS version + tool versions + total wall-clock.
- Fixture validation failures log the offending file + the schema that rejected it.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §16 Phase 0 — research spike tasks.
- §18.3 — adapter contract tests.
- §17 — references-list of every wrapped tool.
- hp-r7i / hp-jvm / hp-6v3 / hp-78m / hp-wle / hp-d54 — Phase 0 implementation beads.
- hp-qrk / hp-lxs / hp-6sv / hp-q3t — substrate.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- Every fixture validates against schema in CI.
- Snapshot-script output reproducibility verified across 100 runs.
- Fixture-completeness gate: missing fixture = CI fail.
- Per-tool contract docs cover all entries in §17 references.
- Manual-wizard fixtures committed with both happy + failure paths.
close_reason: validateCorpus() + 14-assertion bun-test suite + scripts/test/validate-fixtures.sh shell wrapper. Asserts completeness, parseability, shape, failure-path coverage, no Guardrail-11 secrets. health adapter added to ADAPTERS list (was off-by-one vs kinds.ts). 14/14 bun tests pass; shell wrapper PASS; typecheck clean.

title: Phase 5: Subscription-window usage in plan-job UI (caut-driven, not token dollars)
description:
WHAT
====
Per-plan-job UI element showing 'active model + elapsed time + estimated remaining' AND per-project subscription-window usage from caut. Token-level cost is NOT primary because subscription-backed CLIs and Oracle don't cost tokens — they cost RATE-LIMIT BUDGET on the user's subscription, which is what caut meters.

WHY (§7.1 'Plan round cost and time discipline')
==================================================
Hoopoe is subscription-only (§13). The metric tracked is per-provider subscription quota (Claude Max / GPT Pro / Gemini Ultra / ChatGPT Pro), not per-token API dollars. UI surfaces 'X% of daily Claude Max quota' rather than dollar precision. If caut isn't reporting for a provider, label 'unmeasured' rather than fabricate.

SHAPES TO CAPTURE PER MODEL CALL (§7.1)
=========================================
- per-call wall-clock latency
- model identity
- harness (Claude Code / Codex CLI / Gemini CLI / Oracle)
- CAAM account when applicable
- input hash
- artifact hash

RENDERED IN UI
================
- Plan-job sidebar: 'Active: ChatGPT Pro via Oracle (claude-max.account.alpha) — 8m 42s elapsed, ~5m remaining'.
- Per-project usage view (top-bar pill, §7.6) — subscription-window usage per provider.
- 'If extended-reasoning Oracle run is consuming user's daily Pro quota, UI says that explicitly.'

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §7.1 — cost discipline.
- §7.6 — top-bar subscription pill.
- §13 — subscription requirement.
- §17 caut reference.
- audit log — per-call records preserved.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- caut adapter reads per-provider usage snapshots.
- Plan-job UI shows latency + estimated remaining + active CAAM account.
- 'unmeasured' shown when caut not reporting.
- No dollar amounts in plan UI for subscription-backed runs.
- Top bar subscription pill (Phase 4 wired seed) gets full caut data here.

title: Phase 8: Swarm launch orchestration (reconcile → gates → warnings → spec → NTM spawn → stagger → kickoff → tending activate)
description:
WHAT
====
The full swarm-launch sequence per §7.3 'Launch sequence'.

SEQUENCE (§7.3)
=================
1. Reconcile project state (canonical wins, §1.1).
2. Verify launch gates per §4.2:
   - VPS ready
   - Project imported
   - Plan locked
   - Beads created + finalized
   - Launch ready (NTM healthy, Agent Mail healthy, bv healthy, br ready --json nonempty or intentionally scoped, build queue policy set)
3. Show warnings:
   - dirty Git
   - stale reservations
   - no ready beads
   - low disk
   - missing Agent Mail
   - **N unpushed commits on VPS**
4. Create swarm spec + audit event.
5. Call NTM spawn / add for each agent.
6. STAGGER agent starts ≥30s apart (§7.3 default policy + thundering-herd guidance).
7. Send kickoff prompt (§7.3 template — separate task).
8. Start event subscriptions (NTM panes, Agent Mail, br updates).
9. Activate the project's tending jobs (§8) — Phase 10 wires fully.
10. Show swarm dashboard.

DEFAULT LAUNCH POLICY (§7.3)
==============================
- Stagger starts ≥ 30s.
- Force AGENTS.md and README reread.
- Require Agent Mail registration.
- Require bv --robot-triage and br ready --json before claiming work.
- Mark claimed beads in_progress.
- Reserve files before edits.
- Include bead ID in mail subjects, reservation reasons, commit messages.
- Use rch for builds/tests when configured.
- Never invoke bare bv.
- Avoid concurrent builds for same project.
- Self-review with fresh eyes before review/close.
- Report blockers quickly.
- Do not wait in communication purgatory.

POST-COMMIT AUTO-PUSH HOOK INSTALL (§7.3)
============================================
Phase 4 task installs the hook; this bead enables it at swarm-launch and removes at swarm-halt.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §7.3 — launch sequence + default policy.
- §4.2 gate invariants.
- Phase 4 auto-push hook task.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- Launch sequence complete end-to-end.
- All warnings displayed before launch.
- Audit event written for swarm spec creation + every agent spawn.
- Stagger respected (≥30s).
- Auto-push hook installed at launch / removed at halt.
- Tending activation (Phase 10 wires fully) — Phase 8 leaves a hook to invoke.

title: Phase 2.5: Bounded-channel + slow-consumer load tests (Appendix B anti-pattern #1)
description:
WHAT
====
Load tests asserting that no daemon channel is unbounded: synthetic high-volume terminal output paired with deliberately slow client must trigger _lag emission within bounded latency, must NOT cause daemon memory blowup, and must allow recovery via /v1/events/replay or chunked log fetch.

WHY (Appendix B 'Anti-patterns to refuse' #1)
==============================================
T3code uses PubSub.unbounded everywhere — a memory-leak landmine when slow consumers sit on fast producers (terminal output, activity stream). Hoopoe's daemon must bound channels at design time. This test catches regressions.

TEST SCENARIOS
==============
1. **High-volume terminal output, slow renderer**
   - Job emits 100 MB of stdout in under 60s.
   - Renderer subscribes via WS but acks events very slowly (or backpressures).
   - Assertion: daemon RSS does NOT grow unboundedly; _lag event emitted with lastPersistedOffset; /v1/jobs/{id}/log?offset=<n> serves the missing range.
2. **Wedged WS consumer**
   - Renderer connects then stops reading without disconnecting.
   - Assertion: daemon detects the hang within bounded time; closes the WS; resources released.
3. **Activity stream burst**
   - 10,000 events in 10s on the activity channel.
   - Assertion: events delivered in order with sequence cursors; older events spilled to persisted log; client reconnect can fetch missed events.
4. **Bounded semaphore stress**
   - Trigger more concurrent llm_calls / git_ops / build runs than the cap allows.
   - Assertion: excess requests queue or 429; no cap violation observed.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §10.1 — backpressure, lag, replay rules.
- §10.5 — SLO targets.
- Appendix B — anti-patterns.
- Phase 2.5 chaos suite.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- Load test suite committed under apps/daemon/test/load/.
- All four scenarios pass with the SLO targets met.
- Daemon RSS stays within bounded growth (e.g., < 256 MB for 100 MB stream test).
- CI runs at least the smoke versions on every PR; full load test runs nightly.

title: Fixture-replay harness over Mock Flywheel Mode (deterministic boot + scenario library + assertion helpers)
description:
WHAT
====
A test-only harness that takes a Mock Flywheel fixture corpus (hp-o74) and boots a complete daemon-equivalent state in-process for fast, deterministic phase-test execution. Tests choose a scenario name; the harness boots the corresponding snapshot, replays the event stream up to a chosen sequence number, and exposes typed RPC handles for assertions.

WHY
====
Without this harness:
- Every phase test invents its own way to set up state.
- 'Daemon restart at X' becomes 'kill the process and pray it comes back the same way.'
- Reproducibility gets accidentally broken by relying on real time, real network, or real PID ordering.

This is the difference between 'we have e2e tests' and 'our e2e tests fail consistently when the bug is real and pass consistently when the bug is fixed.'

SCENARIO LIBRARY (initial)
============================
- happy.empty-project — fresh project, no plan, no beads.
- happy.locked-plan — project with a locked plan, ready for bead conversion.
- happy.beads-ready — project with beads, ready set non-empty.
- happy.swarm-running — 4 agents running, mid-bead, mail traffic.
- happy.review-mode — implementation done, in review rounds.
- failure.malformed-bead — br --json returns malformed entry.
- failure.ntm-disconnected — NTM serve unreachable.
- failure.tunnel-flapping — SSH tunnel up/down/up at fixed intervals.
- failure.daemon-restart-mid-job — job runs across simulated daemon restart.
- failure.skill-drift — installed skill SHA != lock file pin.
- failure.postcondition-failure — typed action executes but canonical state shows no effect.
- failure.budget-breach — caut reports >90% of daily Claude Max quota.
- failure.disk-pressure — srp signals 95% disk full.
- failure.stale-reservation — reservation past TTL with no owner activity.
- failure.rate-limited-with-caam — rate-limit signal + CAAM has alternate account.
- failure.rate-limited-no-caam — rate-limit signal + no alternate account.
- chaos.commit-burst — 50 commits in 10 seconds, snapshot-health must coalesce.
- chaos.activity-flood — 10k Activity events in 1 minute, slow renderer.

DETERMINISM RULES
==================
- All time inside the harness comes from a virtual clock (advance-by-API).
- All randomness has seeded RNG.
- All network calls are mocked at adapter boundary (no actual SSH, no actual HTTP).
- All file IO points at a temp dir reset per test.
- The same fixture + same scenario + same seed produces byte-identical evidence files.

ASSERTION HELPERS
===================
- expectEvent(channel, filter): waits for a matching event up to virtual-time deadline.
- expectAuditEntry(filter): asserts an entry was written with the expected shape.
- expectActionPlanShape(plan): validates a typed ActionPlan against tending-actions.yaml.
- expectPostcondition(action, predicate): runs the postcondition predicate against canonical state.
- expectNoLeak(): asserts no goroutines, file handles, or DB connections leaked.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §13 — Mock Flywheel Mode is required for development and demos.
- §8.8 — tending evaluation harness consumes this for replay/eval.
- hp-o74 — Mock Flywheel wiring (substrate).
- hp-wle — Phase 0 fixture corpus (input).
- hp-rnn — Phase 10 tending evaluation harness (consumer).
- hp-qrk — parent epic.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- Harness shipped under apps/daemon/test/replay/ with mirrored helpers in apps/desktop/test/.
- All 18 initial scenarios committed under packages/fixtures/scenarios/.
- Determinism gate: same scenario run 100× produces the same evidence hash.
- Documented in docs/testing.md as the canonical phase-test substrate.
close_reason: Implemented in aea43ac — @hoopoe/fixture-replay package + bootMockFlywheel/expectStageReached/expectAdapterCalled/getEmittedEvents/assertNoUnredactedSecrets + 9 e2e tests across fresh/active/failure scenarios; 300 desktop tests still green via rch + bun

title: Phase 7: Kanban + DAG + bead-drawer test suite (DAG layout correctness, critical path, ready frontier, 500-node clustering, dependency editing, cycle warnings)
description:
WHAT
====
Phase 7 test suite covering the Kanban view, DAG view (React Flow), the bead detail drawer, dependency editing UX, and the bv --robot-triage panel.

UNIT TESTS
============
- Kanban: status columns (ready / claimed / in_progress / in_review / closed / blocked); drag-to-claim disabled by default (claiming is an audited daemon RPC, not a UI gesture); filter chips (priority, owner, label, search); bulk select with bulk-update guarded by approval.
- Bead card rendering: title, ID, priority chip, status pill, owner agent, files-touched count, time-on-bead, linked plan section.
- DAG view (React Flow): top-down topological layout by longest-path-from-root; sibling order within a layer follows average parent x-coordinate to minimize edge crossings; critical path + ready frontier highlighted; 500-node clustering when N > 500 visible nodes (cluster-by-priority, then by phase, then by label).
- Bead detail drawer: 9 panels (overview, full context, dependencies, plan traceability, mail thread, files / reservations, tests / health, commits, review findings, audit history); each panel reads from canonical sources via daemon RPCs; updates live via WS events.
- Dependency editing: br dep add / remove fires through daemon RPC; cycle warning surfaces immediately (br dep cycles --json runs after every change); failed cycle reverts the change with audit.
- bv --robot-triage panel: top-pick beads, actionable_count, blocked_count, open_count, recipe filtering (actionable, parallel, etc.).
- Rounds modal: exposes the literal prompt sent to each polish-round model alongside the action summary.

E2E TESTS
==========
1. Render fixture graph (500 nodes) → critical path highlighted + ready frontier visible + clustering activates beyond 500 (Phase 7 acceptance + @slo dag.usable.500-nodes).
2. Bead Kanban load with 1,000 beads → @slo bead.kanban.load.1k.p95 ≤ 1s holds.
3. Open bead drawer → all 9 panels populate from canonical sources within 2s.
4. Add dependency between two beads → cycle check passes → daemon RPC fires → audit event + UI updates.
5. Add dependency that creates a cycle → preview blocks the action with a clear message + linked cycle path.
6. Filter chip 'priority=0' → Kanban + DAG re-render with only P0 beads; counters update.
7. bv --robot-triage panel populates with real bv output; click top pick → drawer opens.
8. Bead status transitions in Kanban respect daemon-RPC-only gate (UI cannot bypass).

CHAOS / FAULT INJECTION
========================
- DAG with deliberately wide layer (N > 1000 siblings) → layout falls back to clustering without OOM.
- Bead drawer opened on a bead deleted mid-render → graceful 'not found' state, no crash.
- bv --robot-triage timeout → adapter capability degrades; panel shows 'unavailable' explanation.
- Real-time WS event burst (100 bead.changed events in 1s) → coalescing keeps UI responsive; no dropped state.

LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
======================
- DAG layout duration logged per render with node-count + edge-count.
- Critical-path computation logs the chosen path + length.
- Cycle-detection failures log the offending cycle path.
- Drawer open logs the bead ID + correlationId for follow-up RPC traces.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §7.2 — Beads stage views.
- §16 Phase 7 — engineering tasks.
- §18.1 Phase 7 acceptance row.
- §10.5 — DAG usable target + bead-Kanban load target.
- design/mockups/v1/beads.jsx — DAGView + RoundsModal references.
- hp-hbq / hp-s2x / hp-0ba / hp-xn3 — Phase 7 implementation beads.
- hp-qrk / hp-lxs / hp-6sv / hp-q3t — substrate.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- Unit coverage ≥80% on Kanban + DAG components + dependency editor.
- All 8 e2e scenarios pass.
- @slo dag.usable.500-nodes + bead.kanban.load.1k.p95 pass on reference hardware (M-series Mac).
- Storybook visual regressions cover all priority/status/owner combinations.

title: Phase 3: Structured ACFS bootstrap parser (§6.4) with raw-log fallback
description:
WHAT
====
Wrap the canonical ACFS installer with a phase parser that emits structured events when stable markers are seen. Falls back to raw-log mode if markers change or parsing confidence drops, while still preserving the run + exit code + resume action.

WHY (§6.4 'Structured ACFS bootstrap parsing')
================================================
ACFS remains the canonical installer. Hoopoe wraps it but must NOT depend on brittle cosmetics. If a future ACFS update changes log format, the wizard must degrade to raw logs without losing resume-from-checkpoint behavior.

EVENT MODEL (§6.4)
====================
phase.start       {phase, name}
phase.line        {phase, stream, offset, text}
phase.checkpoint  {phase, key, status}
phase.end         {phase, rc, durationMs}
phase.fail        {phase, rc, lastLines, resumeHint}

BOOTSTRAP RUNNER
=================
- Records every line to ~/.hoopoe/logs/bootstrap-{runId}.log (per §2.7 persistent logs).
- Streams the canonical curl|bash one-liner with --yes --mode vibe --ref <pinned-tag>.
- Detects stable markers and emits structured events.
- When markers are missing or parsing confidence drops, sets parser.confidence=low and surfaces raw log to wizard UI.

PHASE FIXTURES (§6.4 'Phase 0 must capture')
=============================================
- clean install
- partially-complete install (resume scenario)
- failed dependency install (network/permission)
- already-installed ACFS run (idempotent)

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §6.3 — bootstrap sequence (this is the implementation behind step 4 of that sequence).
- §6.4 — parser model.
- Phase 0 fixtures — input source for the parser tests.

CONSIDERATIONS
==============
- Parser must NEVER block on a missing marker; degrade to raw-log mode.
- Phase markers should be pinned to ACFS release tag in the parser's marker library; bumping ACFS bumps the marker library too.
- Fail-resume: phase.fail event includes resumeHint pointing the user to /v1/bootstrap/acfs/resume.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- Parser handles all 4 fixture scenarios (clean, partial, failed-dep, already-installed) correctly.
- Marker mismatch test: synthetically corrupted log triggers raw-log fallback; UI still shows progress.
- /v1/bootstrap/acfs/start streams structured events; /v1/bootstrap/acfs/resume picks up from last completed phase.
- Bootstrap log file persists past the run for forensic inspection.

title: Cross-cutting: Test + observability infrastructure (structured logging, reporters, fixture replay, coverage gates)
description:
WHAT
====
Cross-cutting epic for the engineering infrastructure that makes 'comprehensive unit + e2e tests with great, detailed logging' (per the polish criterion) actually executable. This is NOT the per-phase test work — those beads live under each phase epic. THIS epic owns the substrate they all build on:

1. Structured-logging library (desktop + daemon) with consistent shape, redaction, level routing, and per-test capture.
2. Test runner config + reporters (vitest for desktop/TS, go test + gotestsum for daemon, playwright for app-level e2e).
3. Coverage gates per package with HTML + lcov + per-PR delta enforcement.
4. Fixture-replay harness sitting on top of Mock Flywheel Mode (hp-o74) so any phase test can boot a fixture corpus deterministically.
5. Test evidence persistence — per-run JSON evidence under docs/test-evidence/<phase>/<timestamp>/ and per-PR rollup.
6. Failure repro context: every failed assertion auto-captures Git SHA, daemon version, fixture scenario, exact step, recent log slice.
7. SLO regression gates: §10.5 numeric targets enforced as test thresholds in CI (e.g. desktop reconnect p95 ≤ 10s, bead Kanban load p95 ≤ 1s).

WHY
===
The user's polish ask is explicit: 'comprehensive unit tests and e2e test scripts with great, detailed logging so we can be sure that everything is working perfectly after implementation.' Without a substrate, every phase invents its own logging shape, its own assertion failure format, its own coverage definition, and tests become unreliable noise instead of a regression net. The whole point of building this foundation early — even though it has no end-user feature — is that the rest of the codebase compounds on it.

This is also the structural answer to §10.5 (SLO + fault injection) and §18 (verification matrix): both demand a measurement substrate that does not currently exist anywhere in the bead graph as a discrete deliverable.

CHILD BEADS (created under this epic)
======================================
- Structured logging library (desktop TS + daemon Go) with redaction sink and test-capture transport.
- Test runner config + reporters + JSON evidence emitter.
- Coverage gates + per-PR delta enforcement.
- Fixture-replay harness over Mock Flywheel Mode.
- SLO assertion library + threshold enforcement in CI.
- Per-phase unit + e2e test beads (Phase 1, 2, 2.5 extension, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 11, 12) — each owns its own test surface but shares this substrate.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §10 — observability and recovery (logging is half of observability).
- §10.5 — SLO targets that become test thresholds.
- §14 — every risk is also a test scenario.
- §18 — verification matrix tied to the per-phase test beads.
- Appendix C #10 — '[SILENT] does not suppress audit'; the same rule applies here: test logs are always retained.
- hp-m42 — high-level coverage targets (desktop ≥80%, daemon ≥85%, schemas 100%); this epic provides the engine.
- hp-o74 — Mock Flywheel Mode (the fixture-replay foundation).
- hp-2qn — chaos suite (one consumer of this substrate).
- hp-8a8 — adapter contract tests (another consumer).

CONSIDERATIONS
==============
- The structured-logging shape MUST match the audit-log shape (cross-cutting hp-g73) so a test failure log can be cross-referenced against an audit entry without translation. Same field names, same redaction rules.
- Coverage gates should fail PRs that REGRESS coverage, not just PRs that fail to hit targets. A regression-only gate is more useful than a fixed bar.
- The fixture-replay harness must be deterministic (no real time, no real network) — tests are reproducible only if all randomness is captured in the fixture.
- Test evidence persistence is bounded: keep last 30 days of per-run evidence + every release's evidence permanently. Without a retention policy this directory grows without bound (this is exactly what §10.3 retention rules try to prevent for the audit log; same principle).
- Logging-level routing must redact secrets at the source, not at the sink — a test run that fails should not leak bearer tokens, SSH passphrases, model keys, or browser cookies into evidence files.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- Structured-logging library shipped + integrated in desktop and daemon.
- vitest (desktop) + go test/gotestsum (daemon) + playwright (e2e) all emit structured JSON logs with the agreed shape.
- Coverage gates active in CI (per-PR delta enforcement).
- Fixture-replay harness can boot any committed fixture corpus and run a phase test against it.
- SLO assertion library wired to §10.5 targets; CI fails on regression.
- Per-phase test beads under this epic depend on the substrate above (no per-phase test bead may invent its own logging shape).
- docs/testing.md + docs/observability.md describe the substrate and how to consume it.

title: Cross-cutting: capability registry + degraded-mode contract
description:
WHAT
====
A daemon-side registry that gates every UI feature by CAPABILITIES, not by optimistic assumptions about installed tool versions. Each adapter (NTM, br, bv, Agent Mail, Git, Health, CAAM, caut, DCG, jsm/jfp, oracle) reports its capabilities. The desktop never assumes a feature exists because a tool is installed.

WHY (§2.8)
==========
Adapters can parse output successfully and still not satisfy a capability. A fixture that parses but cannot satisfy the capability must NOT mark the feature as available. This is the difference between 'the binary is installed' and 'the operation actually works on this VPS at this version with this configuration'.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §2.6 — GET /v1/capabilities and GET /v1/compatibility
- §2.8 — full registry shape
- §7 stage features — every major UI button declares the capability IDs it requires
- §8.2 — tending jobs: capabilities_required and capabilities_optional with degraded_mode policy
- §10.2 — Diagnostics explains the exact missing capability and the fallback in use

REGISTRY SHAPE
==============
{
  tool: 'ntm'|'br'|'bv'|'agent_mail'|'git'|'ru'|'caam'|'caut'|'dcg'|'casr'|'pt'|'srp'|'sbh'|'ubs'|'jsm'|'jfp'|'oracle'|'health_<lang>',
  version: semver,
  source: 'ntm serve' | '--robot-* JSON' | 'CLI' | 'fixture' | ...,
  capabilities: {
    <capId>: {status: 'ok'|'degraded'|'missing'|'blocked-by-policy', fallback?, transport?}
  },
  lastCheckedAt: RFC3339,
  fixturesVersion: <phase0-fixture-tag>
}

CAPABILITY ID NAMESPACES (illustrative)
========================================
- ntm.sessions.list, ntm.panes.stream, ntm.approvals.list, ntm.robot.snapshot, ntm.swarm.halt
- br.issues.read, br.issues.update, br.dep.add
- bv.robot.triage, bv.robot.plan, bv.robot.insights, bv.robot.diff
- agent_mail.messages.read, agent_mail.messages.send, agent_mail.reservations.list
- git.status.read, git.diff.read, git.push, git.unpushed.list
- caam.accounts.list, caam.account.switch
- caut.usage.snapshot
- dcg.verdicts.subscribe
- casr.session.resume
- pt.kill, srp.signals.read, sbh.cleanup
- ubs.scan
- jsm.skill.install, jsm.skill.verify, jfp.skill.install
- oracle.serve.status, oracle.browser.run
- health.coverage, health.complexity, health.churn

DEGRADED-MODE CONTRACT (§2.8, §8.2)
====================================
For each tending job (and every UI feature):
- if_missing_required: block_job | run_read_only | emit_diagnostic
- if_missing_optional: continue_with_warning | suppress_related_detections
- activity_behavior: silent | diagnostics_only | activity_panel_warning

UI BEHAVIOR
===========
The desktop renders one of: available | degraded | unavailable | blocked-by-policy. Diagnostics explains the exact missing capability and fallback. Adapter contract tests assert capabilities, not just parser success.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- /v1/capabilities returns a registry snapshot per the shape above.
- /v1/compatibility composes capability registry + daemon API version + min-desktop version + event schema versions + migration state.
- Every UI feature flag is keyed off declared capabilities; no hard-coded version checks scattered across the renderer.
- Adapter contract tests assert capabilities for normal, missing-tool, unsupported-version, malformed-output, timeout, and high-volume cases (§18.3).
- Fixtures versioned (fixturesVersion: 'phase0-2026-04-30' or later).
close_reason: Implemented in 38d97fe + f5f8a98 + 13107c0 — capability registry + degraded-mode contract per plan.md §2.6/§2.8. Daemon registry (apps/daemon/internal/capabilities/), /v1/capabilities + /v1/compatibility handlers, renderer gate (apps/desktop/src/capabilities/) with FEATURE_CATALOG, OpenAPI schema (locked with hp-r3i in 38d97fe), adapter contract tests for normal/missing/unsupported/malformed/timeout/high-volume + JSON wire-shape contract tests, fixture replay for all 5 Phase-0 scenarios, docs/capabilities.md. CapabilityStatus is 5-valued (ok|degraded|missing|blocked-by-policy|untested); render bucket maps untested→unavailable. UI features key off declared capabilities via FEATURE_CATALOG. Daemon test suite green (29 tests including 7 contract tests + 4 fixture-replay scenarios). Desktop renderer test suite green (26/26 capability tests). Coordinated with RedMountain (hp-r3i) + BrownStone (hp-q3t) in thread hoopoe-phase2.

title: Cross-cutting: packages/schemas — OpenAPI + TS client + Go types
description:
WHAT
====
The single source of truth for all type contracts shared between Hoopoe Desktop (TS) and Hoopoe Server / Daemon (Go). OpenAPI lives in packages/schemas/openapi.yaml; TS client and Go types are GENERATED from it. No hand-maintained duplicate shape definitions across desktop and daemon.

WHY THIS IS A FIRST-CLASS CONCERN
==================================
Every later phase (auth, jobs, projects, Git, plans, beads, swarm, mail, health, reviews, tending, approvals) defines new entity shapes and new endpoints. If we don't formalize the contract early and generate clients from it, drift between desktop and daemon becomes inevitable — and that drift is the entire failure mode this principle prevents (§3 'Shared'). 

Cross-references that touch this epic:
- §2.6 Seed daemon API + WS event contract
- §2.7 Job registry / process manager
- §2.8 Capability registry shape
- §4 Domain model (VPS lifecycle, Project lifecycle, gate invariants)
- Appendix A: §3 'shared' — entity schemas (Project, Plan, Bead, SwarmSession, Agent, FileReservation, BudgetPolicy, BuildQueuePolicy, SwarmLaunchSpec) live here.
- §7 Daemon REST endpoint list
- §8 tending-actions.yaml (typed agent ActionPlan surface)
- §9 finding lifecycle
- §11 packaging — minimum compatible desktop/API versions

DESIGN PRINCIPLES (§3, §10.3)
==============================
- Every persisted table/file has a schema version.
- /v1/compatibility reports daemon API version, minimum desktop version, event schema versions, migration state, unsupported client warnings.
- Event schema changes are versioned (§10.1).
- Adapter contract tests assert capabilities, not just parser success (§2.8).

WHAT GOES IN
=============
1. OpenAPI 3.x spec (packages/schemas/openapi.yaml) covering all Phase 2.5+ endpoints.
2. Generated Go types (packages/schemas/go/) — used by daemon as the source of request/response types, with hand-written validators where OpenAPI alone is insufficient.
3. Generated TS client (packages/schemas/ts/) — used by desktop renderer and main process for all daemon RPCs.
4. WS event envelope schema + per-channel event-type schemas (eventId, schemaVersion, channel, type, sequence, time, actor, causationId, correlationId, data). Sequence-cursor + snapshot-on-reconnect is load-bearing.
5. tending-actions.yaml (typed agent ActionPlan surface) — defines allowed action types: agent.ask_status, agent.send_marching_orders, agent.pause, agent.kill_wedged_process, reservation.force_release, caam.switch_account, casr.resume_session, git.push_branch, swarm.halt, review.propose_flip, bead.create_blocker. Any action type not in the schema is rejected at validation; the agent cannot escape the typed surface by inventing one.
6. Domain entity schemas: VpsHost, Project, Plan, Bead, SwarmSession, Agent, FileReservation, BudgetPolicy, BuildQueuePolicy, SwarmLaunchSpec, PlanQualityScore, BeadSetQuality, CodeHealthSnapshot, FileHealthMetric, CommandSpec, Job, ProviderPluginContract, PaneStreamEvent.
7. Capability registry shape (§2.8): {tool, version, source, capabilities: {capId: {status, fallback, transport}}, lastCheckedAt, fixturesVersion}.
8. Approval entity (§5.3): requested action, actor, reason, evidence, scope, expiry, risk class, decision actor, note.

CONSIDERATIONS
==============
- Generation pipeline must run in CI and pre-commit so drift is impossible.
- Use a generator that emits idiomatic Go (oapi-codegen) and idiomatic TS (openapi-typescript or hey-api). NOT swagger-codegen (low quality output for both).
- WS event schemas are NOT covered by OpenAPI well; use JSON Schema or AsyncAPI for events, and keep both spec files alongside openapi.yaml.
- Each Idempotency-Key, Problem+JSON, and pagination convention is documented once in the spec, not re-explained per endpoint.
- Schema migrations: every persisted shape must declare a schemaVersion field; daemon startup runs migrations with backup and rollback (§10.3).

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- openapi.yaml passes spectral lint with our custom ruleset.
- Generated TS client compiles in apps/desktop and is consumed in at least one IPC handler.
- Generated Go types compile in apps/daemon and are consumed in at least one HTTP handler.
- /v1/compatibility endpoint returns versions matching the spec on a smoke run.
- Schema-version migration test runs in CI: artificial schemaVersion bump migrates DB and rollback works.
- Documented in packages/schemas/README.md.

title: rch adapter (build offload; preferred for builds when configured)
description:
WHAT
====
rch (Remote Compilation Helper) adapter wrapping the canonical rch CLI for build-offload. Preferred build/test execution path when configured per §7.3 swarm rules: 'Use rch for builds/tests when available' and §8.5 build/test contention control.

WHY
===
Heavy concurrent builds in a multi-agent swarm contend for VPS CPU/disk and can wedge the project Git worktree. rch offloads compile/test execution to a dedicated remote worker, freeing the VPS for agent-driven editing + tests. Without an adapter, agents revert to local builds and Hoopoe loses the per-build-job dedupe + caching guarantees the build queue (§2.7) depends on.

This adapter is gated on capability rch.run; when not configured, the build queue falls back to direct go test / cargo / pytest / vitest invocations. Hoopoe never assumes rch presence.

INTEGRATION
=============
- Capability: rch.run (status: ok / degraded / missing / blocked-by-policy).
- Adapter surface: build/test commands, environment digest, rch worker target, result streaming.
- Build queue (Phase 8, hp-977): when rch.run is ok and project policy allows, route the command through rch; otherwise direct.
- Per-result records still capture canonical state: project ID, worktree path, branch, commit SHA, command, normalized argv, environment digest, tool versions, runner profile, lockfile/package-manifest digest, started/completed timestamps, stdout/stderr artifact IDs, parsed test cases + failures + coverage files + exit code, failure fingerprint, cache key + cache hit/miss reason. (See §2.7 build-queue result schema.)
- Failure fingerprints recorded as 'known unchanged failure' across rch and direct paths uniformly so agents stop burning cycles on identical repeated failures.
- Suspected flakes are marked separately and can trigger a flake-hardening bead.

CAPABILITY ID
===============
- rch.run

CONFIGURATION
==============
- Per-project config under .hoopoe/rch.json: worker target, transport, max-concurrency.
- Global default in ~/.hoopoe/userdata/daemon-settings.json.
- Setting changes audited (Hoopoe-policy approval gate for first-time enable).

OBSERVABILITY
==============
- Every rch invocation logs: command, worker target, queue-wait duration, execution duration, exit code, cache hit/miss.
- Failure fingerprints surface as 'known failure' detections to tend-swarm rather than re-running blindly.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §7.3 — swarm rules ('rch when configured').
- §8.5 — build queue + contention.
- §17 — rch reference.
- hp-977 — build/test queue infrastructure (consumer).
- hp-r33 — capability registry (gates the adapter).

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- Adapter implemented + capability rch.run reported.
- Build queue (hp-977) routes to rch when configured + policy allows; falls back cleanly otherwise.
- Adapter contract tests (hp-8a8): happy / missing / unsupported version / malformed output / timeout / high-volume.
- Per-build result schema validated against §2.7.
- Documented in docs/integration-contracts/rch.md (per-tool integration contracts, hp-78m).

title: Phase 0: Provision research-spike VPS with ACFS
description:
WHAT
====
Stand up a 64 GB Ubuntu 24+ VPS with ACFS installed via the canonical curl|bash one-liner (§6.3) on Contabo Cloud VPS 50 / OVH VPS-5 (the agent-flywheel.com top picks per §6.2) or an existing VPS the team already controls.

WHY
===
This VPS is the integration-contract surface. Every Phase 0 deliverable measures something on this machine. It also serves as the live target for Phase 2/3 smoke tests until Phase 13 provider-automation lands.

SPEC (§6.2)
===========
- OS: Ubuntu 24.x or newer (ACFS targets Ubuntu 25.10).
- RAM: 64 GB recommended (warn but do not block at 32 GB).
- vCPU: 12–16.
- Storage: 250 GB+ NVMe SSD.
- Provider: Contabo (~\$56/mo, US datacenter \$10 surcharge) OR OVH VPS-5 (~\$40/mo) OR an existing VPS the team controls. AWS/GCP/Azure are deliberately not recommended (§6.2 — billing unpredictable, 3–5× more expensive).

INSTALL COMMAND (§6.3)
======================
For research-spike use main ToT to capture worst-case parser fixtures:
  curl -fsSL "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Dicklesworthstone/agentic_coding_flywheel_setup/main/install.sh?\$(date +%s)" | bash -s -- --yes --mode vibe

For the production tag pinning Hoopoe will use:
  curl -fsSL "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Dicklesworthstone/agentic_coding_flywheel_setup/<TAG>/install.sh" | bash -s -- --yes --mode vibe --ref <TAG>

CONSIDERATIONS
==============
- Generate a fresh ed25519 key for this VPS; do not reuse personal keys.
- Note the pinned ACFS tag for use in Hoopoe's release manifest (§6.3, §11).
- Capture both 'fresh install' and 'already-installed' run outputs as fixtures (§6.4 phase parser must handle both).

ACCEPTANCE
==========
- VPS reachable via SSH.
- ACFS install completes; acfs doctor --json returns green.
- Canonical CLIs present: br, bv, ntm, agent_mail, rch, caam, dcg, caut, casr, srp, sbh, pt, ubs, jsm or jfp.

title: DCG verdict ingestion adapter (block/requires_confirmation/allowed → unified approvals queue)
description:
WHAT
====
Adapter that subscribes to DCG (Destructive Command Guard) verdict events at the agent boundary and ingests them into Hoopoe's unified approvals queue per §5.3.

WHY (§5.3 'DCG is the agent-side guard; daemon ingests its verdicts')
=======================================================================
Hoopoe does NOT run a parallel guard. DCG (installed by ACFS as Claude Code hook) intercepts dangerous git/fs commands at the agent boundary BEFORE they reach the shell. Hoopoe ingests outcomes (blocked / requires_confirmation / allowed) as approval-source events and merges them into the same approvals queue used for Hoopoe-policy approvals.

WHY THIS MATTERS
==================
The user sees ONE unified approvals UI showing both Hoopoe-policy and DCG-policy gates, each with its source rule attached. This avoids the worst failure mode: two guards with subtly different rules silently disagreeing. DCG-blocked actions get the same audit, approval-scope, and expiry treatment as everything else.

INTEGRATION
=============
- DCG emits structured verdict events; the daemon's DCG adapter (Phase 8) subscribes.
- Each verdict creates an approval-source entry in the unified queue with:
  - source rule: dcg:<rule-id>
  - actor: agent (the agent attempting the command)
  - evidence: command + DCG rule + risk classification
- 'requires_confirmation' state is the user-facing gate; user approves/denies in standard ApprovalDialog UI.
- 'blocked' state is final; not user-overridable (DCG is the spec; if DCG blocks, even autopilot doesn't bypass).

CAPABILITY ID (§2.8)
======================
- dcg.verdicts.subscribe

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §5.3 — DCG ingestion model.
- §6.3 — vibe mode + DCG safety layer.
- approvals queue epic.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- DCG verdict subscriber wired.
- Verdicts ingested into approvals queue with dcg:<rule> source.
- 'blocked' verdicts are final; UI shows them as such (not approvable).
- 'requires_confirmation' verdicts go through standard approval flow.
- Adapter contract tests pass.

title: Electron renderer hardening — contextIsolation + sandbox + nodeIntegration:false + strict CSP + preload typed API + lint rules
description:
WHAT
====
Hard wiring of Electron renderer security per §5.4 lines 1-2. Renderer cannot reach the filesystem, network, SSH, Keychain, or daemon tokens directly. Everything goes through a small typed preload API + main-process IPC handlers that validate schemas and user intent.

CONFIGURATION (BrowserWindow webPreferences)
==============================================
- contextIsolation: true
- sandbox: true (where compatible — fall back to false only with documented justification + audit)
- nodeIntegration: false
- nodeIntegrationInWorker: false
- nodeIntegrationInSubFrames: false
- webSecurity: true
- allowRunningInsecureContent: false
- experimentalFeatures: false
- enableBlinkFeatures: '' (none)

CSP (strict)
=============
default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; connect-src 'self' ws: wss: http://localhost:* http://127.0.0.1:*; img-src 'self' data:; font-src 'self'; object-src 'none'; base-uri 'self'; form-action 'self'; frame-ancestors 'none'.
- inline scripts forbidden (no eval, no Function constructor).
- inline styles allowed only via design system (limited use).
- Dynamic CSP nonces if any inline scripts ever become unavoidable.

PRELOAD TYPED API SHAPE
==========================
window.hoopoe = {
  daemon: { request, subscribe, unsubscribe },
  settings: { get, set, watch },
  keybindings: { compile, dispatch },
  approvals: { listPending, approve, deny, extend },
  files: { openExternal, revealInFinder, ripgrep },
  // every method validates against a schema in packages/schemas/preload-api.yaml
}

LINT RULES (block-on-fail)
=============================
- No fs / net / child_process / electron-renderer-process / ipcRenderer.send-without-typed-wrapper imports in apps/desktop/src/renderer/**.
- No window.require / window.process access.
- No new Function / eval anywhere in renderer.
- No raw <iframe>/<webview>; only allowlisted embed components (none in v1).

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §5.4 lines 1-2 — renderer + preload hardening.
- §14 — 'Lifted code carries Codex-shaped assumptions' risk.
- hp-rkx (parent epic).

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- BrowserWindow config matches above; assertions in main.ts startup test.
- CSP shipped + verified at runtime via meta + HTTP header.
- packages/schemas/preload-api.yaml committed; preload binding verified by typegen.
- Lint rules added; CI fails on violation.
- Test scenario: renderer attempts privileged op → preload rejects + logs the attempt as a security event.
- Documented in docs/security.md as authoritative reference.

title: Phase 12: UBS adapter (first-pass scanner; round 0 + round 5 hotspot-targeted; finding-source stamping)
description:
WHAT
====
UBS (Ultimate Bug Scanner) adapter — fast, deterministic, ACFS-installed first-pass scanner. Runs automatically when entering Debugging/Hardening (Round 0). Re-runs scoped to hotspots in Round 5. Each finding stamped with source: ubs for cross-tool deduping (§9.2).

WHY (§7.4.2, §9.2)
====================
UBS catches bugs a static scanner can find without burning agent cycles. Wiring it as Round 0 + Round 5 means agent-driven rounds attack the harder cases.

INTEGRATION
=============
- Adapter wraps UBS CLI; output JSON parsed into the finding ledger.
- Each finding: {findingId, source: 'ubs', filePath, lineRange, severity, category, message, ruleId, codeContext, time}.
- Cross-tool dedupe: if two sources (UBS + an agent skill) report the same issue at the same file:line, ledger merges with sources: ['ubs', 'skill:<name>'].

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §7.4.2 — Standard tooling.
- §9.2 — review-round catalog.
- §9.5 — specialized audits also stamp source.
- §17 UBS reference.

CAPABILITY ID (§2.8)
======================
- ubs.scan

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- UBS adapter runs against fixture project.
- Findings parsed into ledger with source: ubs.
- Round 0 fires when entering Stage 04.
- Round 5 re-runs UBS scoped to hotspots.
- Cross-tool dedupe tested.

title: Phase 10: Tending evaluation harness + §8.8 fixture suite
description:
WHAT
====
Tending evaluation harness per §8.8. Replay/evaluation suite using Mock Flywheel fixtures. Asserts detections emitted, wake/no-wake behavior, ActionPlan shape + validity, approvals requested, actions executed, postconditions verified or follow-up detections raised, Activity entries emitted, audit records, cost/noise counters.

REQUIRED FIXTURES (§8.8 — already defined by Mock Flywheel epic)
==================================================================
- healthy-hour
- idle-but-not-stuck
- genuinely-wedged-pane
- rate-limited-with-caam
- rate-limited-no-caam (degraded mode)
- stale-reservation
- commit-burst
- budget-breach
- skill-drift
- missing-tool
- postcondition-failure
- action-arbitration

ASSERTIONS PER FIXTURE
=========================
For each scenario:
- Detections emitted match expected.
- Wake/no-wake behavior matches expected.
- ActionPlan shape valid.
- Approvals requested for destructive actions.
- Actions executed.
- Postconditions verified (or follow-up detection raised).
- Activity entries emitted only when warranted.
- Audit records complete.
- Cost (tokens) + noise (panel entries) counters within bounds.

REGRESSION FOR §8.3.1 ACTION SURFACE
=======================================
Adding a new action type to packages/schemas/tending-actions.yaml REQUIRES a fixture that exercises its happy path AND at least one postcondition-failure path. Build this requirement into the test runner.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §8.8 — full fixture list + assertion list.
- Mock Flywheel epic — fixture corpus.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- All §8.8 fixtures committed + runnable.
- Harness asserts every dimension above.

title: Phase 12: Convergence detector + dashboard + final landing checklist
description:
WHAT
====
Convergence detector per §9.4. Dashboard with state machine: not_started → high_yield → medium_yield → low_yield → saturated → final_gate_ready. Final landing checklist (§4.2 'Ship ready' gate).

METRICS PER ROUND (§9.4)
==========================
- Findings per round
- Severe findings per round
- Duplicate findings
- Fixes per round
- New beads per round
- Test failures fixed
- Coverage delta
- Complexity delta
- Cost / time per useful finding

SATURATION CRITERIA (§7.4 'Convergence is the success criterion')
====================================================================
A round is 'saturated' when new useful findings are LOW relative to cost/effort, AND remaining findings are mostly duplicates / low severity / already tracked as beads. The detector tells the user when to ship vs do another round.

FINAL LANDING CHECKLIST (§4.2 Ship ready)
============================================
- tests/builds pass or exceptions documented
- code health gates pass or follow-up beads exist
- Git/beads synced

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §7.4 — convergence as success criterion.
- §9.4 — full state machine + metrics.
- §4.2 — gate invariants.
- §15 — definition of success #13 (convergence visible).

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- Detector computes saturation per round.
- Convergence tab shows state-machine visual + metrics per round.
- Final landing checklist enforced + passable.
- Phase 12 acceptance: review round produces findings; convergence dashboard updates.

title: Phase 5 / §7.1: Existing-codebase context bundle assembly for plan candidates
description:
WHAT
====
The daemon-side subsystem that builds an 'existing-codebase context bundle' and injects it into every candidate-model prompt when the project's repo is non-empty (§7.1 'A third sub-mode applies to both...'). Bundle contents (per §7.1): README, AGENTS.md, architecture docs, package manifests, test layout, existing beads via 'br list --json', and current health hotspots from §7.4.

WHY (§7.1 'A third sub-mode applies to both...')
==================================================
§7.1 states clearly: 'when the project already has a codebase (a non-empty repo), Hoopoe automatically attaches an existing-codebase context bundle ... to the prompts the planning models see. The user gets the same chat-box / import surface either way; the difference is what context the models receive.'

Without this:
- Plan candidates ignore architectural decisions already encoded in the codebase. New plans propose patterns that contradict the existing layer cake.
- Test layout (vitest vs jest, location of fixture data, mock conventions) gets re-invented per plan.
- Package manifests' constraints (peer deps, Node version, OS support) get violated.
- Existing-bead overlap. The new plan re-proposes work the swarm has already started or finished.
- Health hotspots get ignored. The new plan's first beads land on top of the worst-coverage / highest-complexity files without flagging the risk.

This is a quality-of-plan input that DIRECTLY drives whether bead conversion (§7.2) produces useful work. It MUST be a separate, testable, version-pinnable subsystem — not buried in the plan-pipeline orchestrator (hp-al4).

ARCHITECTURE
=============

BUNDLE STRUCT
--------------
ExistingCodebaseContextBundle struct {
  ProjectId       ProjectID
  CommitSHA       string                   // pinned at bundle-creation time
  GeneratedAt     time.Time
  ContentHash     string                   // sha256 of normalized contents

  Readme          *FileSnapshot            // README.md / README.rst / etc., capped to 50KB
  AgentsMd        *FileSnapshot            // AGENTS.md if present
  ArchitectureDocs []FileSnapshot          // docs/architecture/* + ARCHITECTURE.md, capped 100KB total
  PackageManifests []ManifestSnapshot      // package.json, pyproject.toml, Cargo.toml, go.mod, etc.
  TestLayout      *TestLayoutSummary       // detected runner, test file patterns, fixture conventions, coverage config
  ExistingBeads   []BeadSummary            // br list --json --limit 0 (truncated descriptions, full IDs/titles/deps)
  HealthHotspots  []HotspotSummary         // top 25 hotspots by composite score (from §7.4.1)

  Excluded        []string                 // paths excluded due to size/policy/secret-scan
  Redactions      []RedactionEntry         // any redactions applied per §5.5 model-context policy
  TokenEstimate   int                      // estimated tokens
  TokenBudget     int                      // configured per-prompt budget
}

ASSEMBLY PIPELINE
------------------
On 'Generate plans' click:
1. **Discover** — walk project root for README*, AGENTS.md, docs/architecture/, ARCHITECTURE.md, manifests; cap walk depth.
2. **Detect test layout** — heuristic: presence of vitest.config / jest.config / pytest.ini / pyproject test config / cargo.toml [test]/ go test pattern; surface as detected runner + sample test file paths.
3. **Pull beads** — daemon BrAdapter call to 'br list --json --limit 0'. Truncate descriptions per bead to 200 chars (full content as artifact link).
4. **Pull hotspots** — daemon HealthAdapter call for top 25 by composite score.
5. **Apply model-context policy** (§5.5) — exclude per .hoopoe/model-context-policy.json (excluded paths, secret-scan blocks/redactions, max context size).
6. **Token budget** — estimate tokens per section; truncate from least-load-bearing first (test layout details > architecture docs > existing beads); HARD CAP at configured budget per candidate model.
7. **Hash + persist** — write the bundle to .hoopoe/plans/<plan-id>/context-bundle-<sha>.json + record sha in meta.json.
8. **Inject into prompts** — each candidate model sees the bundle as a structured 'context' section in their prompt template.

PER-MODEL ADAPTATION
---------------------
Some models prefer different framing (e.g., Claude likes explicit XML-tagged context; ChatGPT Pro Oracle handles markdown-fenced blocks; Gemini handles either). The candidate-model dispatcher (hp-du6 / hp-xr8) requests the bundle in the format its target model prefers — the bundle struct is canonical, the SERIALIZER is per-model.

CACHE
======
Bundles are content-addressable by ContentHash. If the same project at the same commit SHA + same model-context policy + same brand of bead/hotspot/manifest produces the same bundle, reuse it across multiple plan jobs (e.g., during refinement rounds the bundle doesn't need re-assembly). Bundle eviction: LRU with 30-day cap.

UI
==
- Plan job 'Context bundle' artifact in the artifact rail. User can preview the bundle (collapsed sections, expand on click).
- 'Manage what models see' link goes to the project's .hoopoe/model-context-policy.json editor (Settings → Project).
- If the bundle hits token budget cap and had to truncate, banner: 'Bundle truncated — N items excluded; [view what was excluded]'.
- Per-candidate plan job log surfaces: 'used context bundle <sha>' so user can correlate plan quality back to bundle versions.

UNIT TESTS (with detailed structured logging)
==============================================
1. **Discovery on standard layout** — fixture project with README + AGENTS.md + docs/architecture/foo.md + package.json + vitest.config.ts; assert all 5 discovered. Log: discovery walk + matched paths.
2. **Discovery handles missing files** — fixture project with ONLY package.json; assert bundle has nil Readme, nil AgentsMd, empty ArchitectureDocs but populated PackageManifests. Log: discovery + bundle struct.
3. **Test layout detection — multi-runner** — fixture with vitest.config.ts AND pytest.ini AND go.mod; detect all three; surface as 'multi-runner project'. Log: detection signals.
4. **Manifest parsing** — package.json with deps + peerDeps + engines; assert parsed into ManifestSnapshot with all three. Log: input + parsed.
5. **Bead truncation** — bead with 5KB description; assert truncated to 200 chars in bundle but description-artifact-link included. Log: input length, truncated content, artifact link.
6. **Hotspot ranking** — fixture with 100 hotspots; assert top 25 by composite score selected. Log: input scores + selected.
7. **Model-context policy excludes** — policy excludes secrets/**; bundle excludes secrets/foo.json with redaction entry. Log: policy, excluded path, redaction entry.
8. **Token budget hard cap** — bundle naturally weighs 60k tokens; budget=30k; truncate from least-load-bearing; assert TokenEstimate ≤ 30k AND README + AGENTS.md preserved. Log: pre-truncation size, truncation order, post-truncation size.
9. **Cache hit** — generate bundle for project at SHA X with policy Y; immediately request again; assert cache hit (same ContentHash returned without re-walk). Log: 1st request timing + 2nd request timing + cache hit log.
10. **Per-model serialization** — same bundle requested as 'claude-xml' and 'markdown'; assert different serialization shapes; canonical struct unchanged. Log: per-format serialized output.

E2E TESTS (with detailed structured logging)
=============================================
1. **Existing-codebase plan generation end-to-end** — fixture project (Hoopoe itself, ~50k LOC) with full doc/test layout; user types rough idea in chat box; click 'Generate plans'; verify each candidate model receives a bundle; bundle.ContentHash matches across all 4 candidate jobs; per-candidate log surfaces 'used context bundle <sha>'. Log: full plan-generation timeline + bundle-injection events.
2. **Empty project skip** — fresh empty-repo project; bundle assembly returns nil (no codebase to bundle); plan candidates run with rough_idea ONLY (no bundle injection). Log: discovery returning empty + plan job log absence of bundle ref.
3. **Truncation surfaced to user** — fixture project with massive docs/ (200KB) + 500 beads; bundle hits budget cap; UI banner 'truncated — 320 beads excluded'; click banner → modal with full exclusion list. Log: pre-truncation manifest, post-truncation, UI banner state.
4. **Model-context policy enforcement** — project policy excludes 'secrets/**' AND blocks if 'AKIA*' string appears; fixture has both; bundle excludes secrets/ AND fails plan job (not silently strip) when AKIA leak detected. Log: policy, fixture, bundle outcome, plan-job decision.
5. **Bundle reuse across refinement rounds** — generate plan; same project SHA; refinement rounds 1-5 all reference the SAME bundle SHA in their per-round logs; no re-assembly cost. Log: per-round bundle ref + cache hit indicators.
6. **Plan quality correlation** — instrument: assert bundle's existing-bead count appears in plan-quality-tracker (hp-s00) 'context richness' dimension; bundle hash linked from plan meta.json. Log: meta.json contents + quality-tracker dimensions.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §7.1 — existing-codebase sub-mode.
- §5.5 — model-context policy.
- §7.4.1 — health hotspots input.
- hp-al4 — multi-model planning pipeline (consumer).
- hp-z5r — chat-box plan input UX (trigger surface).
- hp-du6 — CLI adapters (per-model serialization).
- hp-xr8 — Oracle adapter (per-model serialization for ChatGPT Pro).
- hp-dz8 — br adapter (existing beads source).
- hp-9uh — health adapters (hotspots source).
- hp-l65 — GitAdapter (commit SHA pinning).
- hp-e33 — model-context policy enforcement.
- hp-r3i — packages/schemas (bundle struct + RPC types).
- hp-s00 — plan quality tracker (consumer of bundle stats).
- hp-vh7 — Phase 5 epic parent rollup.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- Bundle struct + serialization in packages/schemas.
- Discovery walk + cap depth.
- Test-layout detection per ecosystem.
- BrAdapter integration (truncation rules).
- HealthAdapter integration (top-25 ranking).
- Model-context policy enforcement.
- Token budget hard cap with documented truncation order.
- Per-model serialization.
- Content-addressable cache with LRU 30d eviction.
- UI: artifact rail bundle viewer + truncation banner + 'manage what models see' link.
- All 10 unit tests + 6 e2e tests pass with detailed structured logs persisted as artifacts.

title: Phase 1: Vendor t3code keybindings + AST + last-rule-wins + add real command registry (no string-switch)
description:
WHAT
====
Lift t3code's keybindings system (~/.hoopoe/keybindings.json, recursive-descent when-clause parser, AST compiled and shipped to client over WS, last-rule-wins, file-watch-debounce). On top of that, build a REAL command registry (Hoopoe addition over t3code).

WHY ADD A COMMAND REGISTRY (§16 Phase 1 week 2 task 2, Appendix B anti-pattern #6)
==================================================================================
T3code's keybindings dispatch via implicit string-switch on the command name. That doesn't scale to per-agent commands, per-tending-job commands, or per-project plugins — exactly what Hoopoe needs. A real registry (commandRegistry.register(id, handler, {whenContextKeys})) lets later phases register commands without modifying renderer/main code.

Also: t3code's anti-pattern #7 — unknown when-clause context keys evaluate to false silently. Hoopoe must validate keys against a known set at parse time, fail loudly on typos.

KEYBINDINGS FILE FORMAT
=======================
~/.hoopoe/keybindings.json:
[
  { "key": "cmd+k", "command": "command-palette.open" },
  { "key": "cmd+1", "command": "stage.planning" },
  { "key": "cmd+2", "command": "stage.beads" },
  { "key": "cmd+3", "command": "stage.swarm" },
  { "key": "cmd+4", "command": "stage.harden" },
  { "key": "cmd+/", "command": "activity.toggle" },
  { "key": "cmd+shift+p", "command": "command-palette.open" },
  ...
]

WHEN-CLAUSE EXAMPLES
=====================
- when: 'stage == swarm && agent.selected'
- when: 'project.locked' (block destructive commands when no active swarm)

CONTEXT KEYS (validated at parse time; unknown = error)
========================================================
- stage: 'planning' | 'beads' | 'swarm' | 'harden' | 'diagnostics'
- project: { active: bool, id, locked: bool }
- agent: { selected: bool, id, status }
- bead: { selected: bool, id }
- approvals: { pending: int }
- swarm: { active: bool, count }

FILES TO LIFT (per Appendix B 'Patterns lifted')
=================================================
- apps/server/src/keybindings.ts → patterns; reimplement in apps/desktop/src/main/keybindings/ (TS, no Effect).
- apps/web/src/keybindings.ts → ditto.

COMMAND REGISTRY (NEW, Hoopoe addition)
========================================
commandRegistry.register(id: string, handler: (ctx) => void, opts?: { whenContextKeys?: string[] })
commandRegistry.dispatch(id: string)
commandRegistry.list({stage?, when?}) — used by the ⌘K palette.

Commands are namespaced: stage.*, swarm.*, bead.*, plan.*, approval.*, diagnostics.*, command-palette.*.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §3 — keybindings + ⌘K palette mention.
- Appendix B — keybindings AST + file watch + last-rule-wins.
- Appendix B 'Anti-patterns to refuse' #6 (string-switch dispatch) and #7 (unknown when-clause keys silently false).

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- ~/.hoopoe/keybindings.json watched + parsed into AST + compiled.
- Unknown context keys cause an error displayed in Diagnostics, not silent false.
- Command registry exposes register / dispatch / list APIs.
- ⌘K palette uses commandRegistry.list to populate.
- Last-rule-wins resolution tested.
- File-watch debounce tested (rapid edits → single reload).
- Unit tests cover parser, AST evaluator, dispatcher.
close_reason: Vendored t3code keybindings parser+evaluator under apps/desktop/src/vendored/t3code/keybindings/ (types/parser/evaluator + tests, MIT attribution preserved); built CommandRegistry (Hoopoe addition: typed register/dispatch + registerContextKey + validateContextKey that throws UnknownContextKeyError on typo, closing Appendix B anti-pattern #6 + #7); KeybindingsManager loads ~/.hoopoe/keybindings.json, validates context keys at parse time, dispatches via CommandRegistry, file-watch + 100ms debounce; default keybindings cmd+1..4 stages, cmd+k palette, cmd+/ activity, cmd+shift+p palette; 30 hp-rth tests pass + 33 prior tests stay green; lint clean for my code (sole repo-wide warning is hp-wle scope); UBS clean

title: Phase 11: Code health metrics test suite (per-language adapters, hotspot scoring, top-bar pill, worktree isolation, snapshot cadence, create-bead-from-hotspot)
description:
WHAT
====
Phase 11 test suite covering health adapters per ecosystem, hotspot scoring, the top-bar code-health pill, worktree-isolated execution, snapshot persistence, and the snapshot-health tending integration.

UNIT TESTS
============
- Health adapters per ecosystem:
  • TS/JS: vitest/jest + lcov + lizard/ts-complexity → unified output shape.
  • Python: pytest + coverage.py + radon/lizard.
  • Rust: cargo test + cargo llvm-cov + lizard.
  • Go: go test -cover + gocyclo/lizard.
  • Generic: configurable shell + lizard/scc/tokei/cloc.
- Coverage parsing: line + branch where supported; per-file + per-function complexity; LOC + effective LOC; churn (last 7/30 days from git log); failed-test counts + durations; ratio of files lacking any test coverage.
- Hotspot scoring: weighted sum of (complexity ≥ 20, coverage < 60%, churn high, recent agent changes, failed tests nearby, review findings nearby, critical-path bead linkage). Thresholds configurable per project; default to spec values.
- Snapshot persistence: each run writes ~/.hoopoe/projects/<id>/health/<run-id>/{report.json, summary.json}; full snapshots + compacted trend history.
- Worktree isolation: every health run uses a dedicated git worktree under ~/.hoopoe/work/<project-id>/health/<run-id>/ at the commit being measured; never the active VPS working tree; if worktree creation blocked, log the reason + schedule retry.
- Snapshot cadence: on every push to main; after each swarm round; on demand from Health tab; from snapshot-health tending job (fires on vps_push_completed + every 10m max); cached results reused if no relevant files changed.
- Top-bar pill: avg coverage %, avg complexity, hotspot count, last-snapshot age; color-coded per project thresholds; click → Health tab; updates within seconds of new snapshot via WS event.
- Per-bead 'files touched' rollup in Beads drawer: current coverage + complexity for each file; delta vs. last snapshot once work begins.
- Per-agent code-health-delta on Swarm agent tile.
- 'health snapshot updated' Activity event with summary (e.g. 'coverage 64% → 67%, 2 new hotspots, 1 hotspot resolved').

E2E TESTS
==========
1. Phase 11 acceptance: health scan runs in a worktree, persists raw + normalized artifacts, updates top-bar pill, can create a bead from a hotspot.
2. Multi-language project (TS + Python + Rust): each adapter runs in its own worktree → unified summary; per-file rows show language-appropriate metrics.
3. Top-bar pill updates after snapshot from any stage; click takes user to Health tab; sortable file table renders 1000-file project quickly.
4. Trend sparklines render correctly with last 30 snapshots; missing-snapshot gaps show explicit gap markers, not interpolation.
5. Create bead from hotspot: click 'Create bead' on a low-coverage/high-complexity file → bead drawer opens with pre-filled title, description (linked file + reason), priority suggestion → user confirms → br create fires through daemon RPC.
6. Worktree-cleanup tending: stale health worktrees older than retention window get GC'd; assert no multi-GB stale dirs after multiple runs.
7. Health worktree blocked by Git state (e.g. worktree limit hit) → snapshot job retries with backoff; user sees clear Diagnostics entry; no in-place run on active working tree.

CHAOS / FAULT INJECTION
========================
- Coverage tool times out → adapter capability degrades; Health tab shows 'last successful snapshot' with timestamp.
- Worktree disk full → snapshot fails with clear error; old worktrees pruned; retry succeeds.
- Adapter crashes mid-run → daemon job marker shows 'failed' + last log slice + reason; user can re-run from Health tab.

LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
======================
- Every health run logs: project ID, commit SHA, language adapters used, per-file durations, total wall-clock.
- Adapter command lines logged with exit codes.
- Worktree create/destroy logged with full path.
- Hotspot scoring logs the weighted-sum components per file (debug level).

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §7.4.1 — code health metrics strategic intent.
- §16 Phase 11 — engineering tasks.
- §18.1 Phase 11 acceptance row.
- hp-9uh / hp-3at / hp-gmm — Phase 11 implementation beads.
- hp-qrk / hp-lxs / hp-6sv / hp-q3t — substrate.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- Unit coverage ≥85% on adapters + hotspot scorer + worktree isolator.
- All 7 e2e scenarios pass.
- Worktree cleanup verified: §10.5 'health worktree cleanup leaves no stale multi-GB directories'.
- Top-bar pill assertions verified across all four stages.

title: Phase 5: Plan quality tracker (intent / architecture / workflow / implementation / testing / risk / bead-readiness)
description:
WHAT
====
Quality tracker that computes per-plan scores along the §7.1 dimensions. Scores are GUIDANCE not truth (§7.1, §14 'Users trust subjective scores too much').

QUALITY DIMENSIONS (§7.1)
===========================
1. Intent clarity — is the goal explicit?
2. Architecture specificity — components, boundaries, data flow.
3. Workflow coverage — user journeys, edge cases.
4. Implementation detail — concrete commands, technology choices.
5. Testing specificity — test plans, evidence requirements.
6. Risk coverage — what could go wrong + mitigations.
7. Bead readiness — ready for Stage 02 conversion?

DETERMINISTIC + MODEL-BASED
=============================
- Deterministic checks: section presence, code-block density, test-plan section presence, decision-list presence.
- Model-based checks: a fast scoring run via the primary model OR a smaller fast model (e.g., Gemini Flash) — runs per round to track convergence.

UI
==
- Quality tracker shows per-dimension score + delta from previous round.
- Linked to artifact viewer: clicking a dimension highlights the relevant plan sections.
- Lock gate uses these scores ADVISORILY — user can lock with low scores after acknowledging.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §7.1 — quality dimensions.
- §14 — risks: 'label scores as decision aids; show underlying evidence'.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- Quality tracker computes scores after each round.
- UI surfaces per-dimension scores with deltas.
- Lock gate reads scores but doesn't block (user can override).
- Underlying evidence visible (not just numbers).

title: ConnectionManager FSM detail — explicit state transitions, triggers, backoff with jitter, sleep/wake hooks
description:
WHAT
====
Concrete implementation of the desktop ConnectionManager FSM described in §2.5: states, transitions, triggers, backoff strategy, sleep/wake hooks. This is more detailed than hp-e7k (SSH profile + tunnel manager) — that bead covers the tunnel substrate; this bead is the higher-level connection-state machine.

STATES
========
- unconfigured — no SSH profile saved.
- ssh_probing — verifying connectivity to the saved profile.
- bootstrapping — running bootstrap.sh to install daemon (if not present).
- tunnel_connecting — establishing local SSH port forward.
- authenticating — exchanging pairing token or refreshing bearer.
- ready — daemon reachable + WS subscribed + cursor in sync.
- degraded — some capability is missing (e.g., NTM serve unreachable but daemon ok).
- reconnecting — tunnel/WS lost; retrying.
- disconnected — explicit user-initiated disconnect; no auto-reconnect.

TRANSITIONS (key)
===================
- unconfigured → ssh_probing: user adds profile.
- ssh_probing → bootstrapping: profile valid, daemon not present.
- ssh_probing → tunnel_connecting: profile valid, daemon present.
- bootstrapping → tunnel_connecting: bootstrap success.
- bootstrapping → unconfigured (with error): bootstrap fails repeatedly.
- tunnel_connecting → authenticating: tunnel up.
- authenticating → ready: bearer + WS-token ok + WS subscribed.
- ready → degraded: capability detected missing (e.g., NTM disconnect).
- ready → reconnecting: tunnel death, WS heartbeat timeout, version mismatch, expired bearer, network change.
- reconnecting → ready: reconnect succeeds.
- reconnecting → disconnected: max-retries exhausted + user dismisses.
- any → disconnected: user-initiated.

TRIGGERS
==========
- macOS sleep (powerMonitor 'suspend' + 'resume').
- Tunnel death (ssh2 process exit).
- Daemon /v1/health failure (3 consecutive failed pings).
- WS heartbeat timeout (30s no _heartbeat).
- Version mismatch (daemon /v1/version returns incompatible).
- Bearer expired (HTTP 401 from daemon).
- Network change (electron netInfo).

BACKOFF
=========
- Initial retry: 1s.
- Exponential: ×2 on each failure, capped at 30s.
- Jitter: ±25% to avoid thundering-herd on multiple desktops connecting to same daemon.
- After 10 failures, transition to disconnected + show user-actionable error.

RECONNECT STEPS (deterministic order)
=========================================
1. Tunnel check: is the local port still forwarding?
2. Bearer/session check: bearer valid?
3. WS-token refresh: mint fresh WS token from /v1/auth/ws-token.
4. Subscribe with sequence cursors: provide latestSequence per channel.
5. Snapshot fetch for any channel that returned _gap.
6. Reconcile local clone if project is active.

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §2.5 — transport ladder + FSM.
- §10 — sequence-cursor + snapshot-on-reconnect.
- §10.5 — desktop reconnect SLO.
- §18 — Phase 2 acceptance test (simulated disconnect/reconnect).
- hp-e7k — SSH profile + tunnel manager (substrate).
- hp-7i1 — AuthBridge.
- hp-wqm — WS sequence cursor + replay.
- hp-iv4 — Phase 2 test bead (consumer).

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- ConnectionManager class implemented under apps/desktop/src/main/connection/.
- Every state + transition tested with the fixture-replay harness (hp-q3t).
- @slo desktop.reconnect.p95 ≤ 10s holds.
- powerMonitor sleep/wake triggers integrated.
- Diagnostics shows current state + last 20 transitions + reason for each.
- docs/reconnect-replay.md includes the FSM diagram + table of triggers.

title: Phase 7: DAG view (React Flow, top-down topological layout, critical path + ready frontier highlight, 500-node clustering)
description:
WHAT
====
React Flow DAG view of bead dependencies. Top-down topological layout (longest-path-from-root layer assignment, sibling order minimizing edge crossings). Highlights critical path + ready frontier. Clustering / virtualization beyond 500 nodes.

LAYOUT (§7.2, design/mockups/v1/beads.jsx DAGView)
=====================================================
- Layer N = longest path from any root.
- Sibling order within layer = average parent x-coordinate (minimizes edge crossings).
- Edges: blocking deps; styled by edge type (blocking / soft / cycle).
- Cycle warnings: red edges + per-cycle warning panel.

CRITICAL PATH HIGHLIGHT
=========================
- Computed from bv --robot-insights or graph-traversal in the renderer.
- Bolded path through highest-priority leaf to root.
- Ready frontier = beads with no open blocking deps (br ready --json).
- Both highlight modes toggleable.

CLUSTERING (§10.5 'DAG usable at 500 visible nodes, clustered beyond')
========================================================================
- Beyond 500 visible nodes, cluster by plan section / label / domain.
- Click cluster to expand.
- Cluster nodes show summary (count of beads inside, status distribution).

EDGE INTERACTION
==================
- Click edge → show edge metadata (added by, added at, why).
- Right-click edge → 'Remove dependency' (audited).

NODE INTERACTION
==================
- Click node → open bead drawer (separate task).
- Hover node → highlight ancestors + descendants.
- Right-click node → 'Add dependency' (drag-to-target).

CROSS-REFERENCES
================
- §7.2 — Views.
- design/mockups/v1/beads.jsx — DAGView reference.
- §3 — React Flow primary; Cytoscape only if needed.
- §10.5 — DAG usable at 500 nodes.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- DAG renders fixture bead graph.
- Critical path + ready frontier highlight toggleable.
- Cycle warnings rendered.
- 500-node fixture usable.
- Edge add / remove operates against PATCH /v1/projects/{id}/beads + br dep add/remove.

title: Phase 11: Code health metrics (top-bar pill light-up; multi-language adapters; hotspots; trend sparklines)
description:
GOAL (§7.4.1, §12 Phase 11)
=============================
First subsystem of Stage 04. Health adapter discovery (TS/JS, Python, Rust, Go, generic); test/coverage/complexity parsing; health snapshots persisted as artifacts; KPI cards; sortable file health table (path, LOC, complexity, coverage bar, churn, owner agent, linked bead, hotspot reasons); hotspot scoring; trend sparklines; create bead from hotspot; LIGHT UP THE TOP-BAR CODE-HEALTH PILL (§7.6) so coverage/complexity/hotspot count are visible from every stage; per-bead 'files touched' health rollup in Beads drawer; per-agent code-health-delta on Swarm agent tile; health snapshot updated events in Activity panel.

WHY 'VISIBLE EVERYWHERE, NOT JUST HERE' (§7.4.1)
==================================================
Code health is a first-class continuous signal, not a once-per-release report. The user must be able to answer 'what is our coverage / complexity / hotspot situation right now?' at any moment from any stage. That's what makes the top-bar pill (§7.6) load-bearing.

EXIT (§12 Phase 11)
=====================
Swarm round updates health; low-coverage / high-complexity files can become beads; user can see project coverage, average complexity, hotspot count from any stage WITHOUT navigating to Health tab.

ACCEPTANCE TEST (§18.1 Phase 11)
==================================
Health scan runs in a worktree, persists raw/normalized artifacts, updates top-bar pill, and can create a bead from a hotspot.

title: Phase 1 component subtask: StatusPill (covers all bead/job/approval/capability/tool states)
description:
WHAT
====
StatusPill component covering every status state in Hoopoe per design tokens. variants: kind = bead | job | approval | capability | tool. sizes: sm | md.

STATES TO COVER
=================
Bead: ready / claimed / in_progress / in_review / closed / blocked / paused
Job: queued / running / waiting_approval / canceling / succeeded / failed / interrupted
Approval: pending / approved / denied / expired / superseded_by
Capability: ok / degraded / missing / blocked-by-policy
Tool: green / yellow / red

ACCESSIBILITY
===============
- AA contrast for every fg-on-bg combination.
- aria-label includes the state name.
- Color is NEVER the only signal — every pill has an icon or shape variant for color-blind users.

DEFINITION OF DONE
==================
- Component implemented + Storybook story per state.
- Contrast tests pass for every state.
- Used in BeadCard + AgentTile + Diagnostics + Activity timeline.
close_reason: Implemented token-backed StatusPill primitive with all required states, story, and tests

title: Network-change adaptation: macOS-specific signal sources (Wi-Fi switch, VPN toggle, captive portal probe) wired into ConnectionManager FSM with debounced reconnect + UI affordances
description:
WHAT
====
Implement macOS-specific network-change detection sources and wire them into the desktop's ConnectionManager FSM (hp-s2cg) as triggers distinct from generic tunnel-drop. Three classes:
1. **Wi-Fi switch / SSID change**: laptop changes networks (e.g., home → coffee shop); SSH tunnel may stay alive but route changes; existing TCP connections may hang silently with no FIN until OS-level keepalive expires (default 2h on macOS).
2. **VPN toggle**: tun0 / utun0 interface appears or disappears; default route flips; firewall rules change; daemon endpoint may become reachable/unreachable abruptly.
3. **Captive portal**: SSH-egress succeeds (port 22 unblocked) but HTTPS to daemon-via-tunnel returns coffee-shop login HTML instead of the expected daemon response.

This bead OWNS:
- macOS network-monitor signal-source layer (Electron `net.online` events; finer signals via SCNetworkReachability through native binding or polling `route -n get default` and `networksetup -listallhardwareports`).
- Captive portal probe protocol (lightweight HTTP GET to `http://captive.apple.com/hotspot-detect.html` matching Apple's standard).
